Our review identifies at least three additional conditions that should have been classified as RECs (the off-site PCE plume (Issue 2), two unregistered USTs (Issue 8), and the former drum storage area (Issue 9)). The Lender’s § 3.B requirements are therefore triggered, and the scope of the required Phase II and cost estimates is broader than the ESA’s two-REC findings would suggest.  
234	          Consequence.         The Borrower faces substantial additional cost and time before closing: a Phase II ESA with quantified cost estimates; a ≥$5,000,000, 10-year PLL policy (premiums can be significant); a possible escrow at 125% of most-likely remediation costs; a possible Ohio-EPA-approved remediation plan; and a vapor intrusion assessment/mitigation plan. The Lender also reserves the right (§ 3.C) to decline the loan if the worst-case remediation cost exceeds 50% of appraised value, if the Property is on the NPL, if there is an active RCRA corrective action order, or if unresolved data gaps prevent adequate risk characterization (see Issue 13). The 36-month remediation completion deadline (if triggered) and the 10-year insurance term extend the Buyer’s obligations well beyond the 3-year APA survival period (Issue 6).

(a) Immediately commission a Phase II ESA scoped to all RECs (including the off-site PCE plume, the two unregistered USTs, and the drum storage area, in addition to the ESA’s two RECs), with the three-scenario cost estimates the Lender requires. (b) Begin PLL insurance underwriting in parallel — it is non-negotiable and on the critical path. (c) Model the escrow and remediation-plan triggers against the cost estimates. (d) Confirm the Lender’s appraisal and the 50%-of-appraised-value decline threshold (§ 3.C(a)) to assess decline risk. (e) Build the Phase II, insurance, escrow, and remediation costs into the deal economics and renegotiate price/indemnity accordingly.

237	        Issue 6 — Seller indemnity package likely inadequate: $1,750,000 cap and 3-year survival period vs. likely remediation scope  
238	          Severity: HIGH  
239	          Description and evidence.         The APA indemnification provisions (Article VIII) provide:         -             Basket:         $87,500 (1% of purchase price) — a true deductible that converts to first-dollar coverage once exceeded. The basket does             not         apply to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) or to covenant breaches or third-party claims (§ 8.2(a)(ii), (iv)) — favorable to Buyer.         -             Cap:         Seller’s aggregate liability under Article VIII             shall not exceed $1,750,000         (20% of the purchase price). This cap             does apply         to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.4(b)).         -             Survival:         The environmental representations (§§ 4.12, 4.13) and the Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities indemnity (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) survive for             three (3) years         after closing (§ 8.4(c)). Claims must be noticed before expiration.         -             Exceptions:         The basket, cap, and survival period do             not         apply to fraud or intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.4(d)).         -             Sole remedy:         Indemnification is the Buyer’s sole and exclusive remedy, except for fraud/intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.2(b)).  
240	      The Lender letter expressly warns (§ 3.B.2) that seller indemnification provisions         “    are typically subject to caps, baskets, deductibles, and survival periods that may be insufficient to cover full remediation costs over the duration of the Bank’s exposure,    ”         and for that reason requires environmental insurance as a non-negotiable supplement.  
241	          Consequence.         The combined remediation scope for this Property — 30+ years of TCE degreasing, 39 years of hexavalent chromium electroplating, an off-site PCE plume, two unregistered USTs, and a former drum storage area — can readily exceed $1,750,000. Chlorinated solvent (TCE/PCE) groundwater remediation alone frequently runs into the millions. If remediation costs exceed the cap, the Buyer bears the excess with no recourse to Seller (absent fraud). The 3-year survival period is also short relative to the timeframe over which groundwater contamination is typically discovered and addressed; contamination that manifests or is discovered after 3 years is barred. The Lender’s required 10-year PLL insurance term and 36-month remediation deadline both extend well beyond the 3-year survival window, meaning the Buyer (and its insurer) will be bearing risk that Seller no longer covers.  
242	          Recommendation.         (a) Negotiate an increase to the indemnity cap and/or a separate, higher environmental-specific cap or carve-out for Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (the basket already does not apply to environmental claims — push for the cap not to apply, or to apply at a higher figure, to environmental claims). (b) Negotiate a longer survival period for environmental representations and Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (e.g., the statutory limitations period for CERCLA, or a minimum of 5–10 years). (c) Require Seller to fund a portion of the PLL insurance premium and/or the remediation escrow as a condition of closing. (d) Preserve all fraud/intentional misrepresentation arguments given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations (Issue 7) — the cap, basket, and survival period do not apply to fraud. (e) Confirm that the Lender’s required PLL insurance and escrow are in place to backstop the gap above the cap.

This is a misclassification that should be corrected to a REC (or, at minimum, a significant data gap requiring investigation).

(b) Document all gaps and the efforts made to close them, as the Lender requires (§ 2). (c) If gaps remain, disclose them to the Lender and assess § 3.C(d) risk. (d) Treat the 1988-spill/ERNS discrepancy as evidence relevant to Seller’s § 4.12(a) and (b) representations.

306	        Issue 14 — Timing mismatch between the diligence/termination deadline and the Phase II/cost-estimate deadline  
307	          Severity: MODERATE  
308	          Description and evidence.         The APA establishes an             Environmental Due Diligence Period ending at 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time on October 31, 2024         (60 days after the September 1, 2024 Effective Date). Under § 6.3(b), if the Phase I ESA identifies RECs that         “    could result in Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000,    ”         the Buyer may terminate and recover its $437,500 earnest money deposit             only by written notice delivered on or before October 31, 2024    . If the Buyer fails to terminate by that date, the right is             automatically waived         (§ 6.3(c)) — though indemnification rights under Article VIII are preserved.  
309	      The Greenfield ESA is dated             October 25, 2024         — only             six days         before the termination deadline. The Lender’s Phase II ESA (with quantified cost estimates) is not due until             November 30, 2024         (15 days before the December 15, 2024 closing; Lender § 5). Thus, the Buyer must decide whether to terminate based on the Phase I ESA alone,             without         the quantified remediation cost estimates that the Lender requires and that the § 6.3(b) standard (    “    Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000    ”    ) implicitly contemplates.  
310	          Consequence.         The Buyer faces a Hobson’s choice: (a) terminate by October 31 based on qualitative Phase I findings (and forfeit the deal), or (b) waive the termination right and proceed, bearing the risk that Phase II cost estimates (available only around November 30) later reveal remediation costs far exceeding $500,000 (or the $1,750,000 indemnity cap). Given the RECs identified (TCE, hexavalent chromium, and the additional RECs we identify — PCE plume, two USTs, drum storage area), remediation costs plausibly exceed $500,000, so a § 6.3(b) termination notice would likely be defensible — but the Buyer would be terminating without cost data. The six-day window is also impractical for legal review of the ESA.  
311	          Recommendation.         (a) Negotiate an extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period (in writing, signed by both parties — § 6.3 requires a written amendment) to allow the Phase II cost estimates to inform the termination decision; tie the extension to the Lender’s November 30 Phase II deadline. (b) If an extension is not granted, evaluate by October 31 whether the qualitative Phase I findings (as supplemented by our additional REC findings) support a § 6.3(b) termination notice, and preserve the right. (c) Note that even if the termination right is waived, Article VIII indemnification (subject to cap/survival) and the fraud exception survive (§ 6.3(c); § 8.4(d)).

313	        Issue 15 — Vapor intrusion risk (TCE, PCE, waste-solvent UST) with non-functional ventilation  
314	          Severity: MODERATE  
315	          Description and evidence.         Three sources of volatile organic compounds are present at or beneath the Property: (a) the former TCE degreasing area (REC-1, with a 42.3 ppm PID reading at a floor crack); (b) the off-site PCE plume migrating toward/onto the Property (Issue 2); and (c) the possible 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST (Issue 8), which, if it held chlorinated solvents, is an additional VOC source. The ESA notes that the ventilation systems in the former degreasing area and the former electroplating room are             non-functional         (disconnected ductwork, sealed exhaust ports). The Property has been used for warehousing since December 2019, meaning workers occupy the building where vapor intrusion could occur.  
316	          Consequence.         Chlorinated solvents (TCE, PCE) are VOCs that migrate from contaminated soil/groundwater into indoor air through vapor intrusion, posing inhalation exposure risks to occupants. The Lender requires a vapor intrusion assessment (§ 3.B.5) and, if warranted, a vapor mitigation plan implemented prior to or concurrently with closing. Vapor intrusion can also create construction-tort and worker-safety exposure for the Buyer as the post-closing owner/occupant.  
317	          Recommendation.         (a) Include a vapor intrusion assessment (sub-slab soil gas and/or indoor air sampling) in the Phase II ESA, focused on the former degreasing area, the former electroplating room, and any occupied warehouse space overlying the PCE plume footprint. (b) If vapor intrusion risks are confirmed, implement a vapor mitigation plan (e.g., sub-slab depressurization) before or at closing, as the Lender requires. (c) Address the non-functional ventilation systems as part of redevelopment planning.

431	        6. PURCHASE AGREEMENT ANALYSIS  
432	       6.1 Termination right (APA § 6.3(b))  
433	      The Buyer may terminate and recover its $437,500 earnest money deposit if the Phase I ESA identifies RECs that         “    could result in Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000,    ”         by written notice on or before             October 31, 2024    . The right is automatically waived if not exercised (§ 6.3(c)); indemnification rights under Article VIII are preserved regardless.  
434	          Analysis.         The ESA’s two RECs (TCE, hexavalent chromium), plus the additional RECs we identify (PCE plume, two USTs, drum storage area), readily support a conclusion that remediation costs         “    could exceed $500,000.    ”         A § 6.3(b) termination notice would be defensible. However, the Buyer would be terminating without the Phase II cost estimates (not due until November 30), and the six-day window between the ESA date (October 25) and the deadline (October 31) is impractical (Issue 14). The Buyer should seek a written extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period.  
435	        6.2 Indemnification (APA §§ 8.2, 8.4)  
436	      Seller indemnifies for breach of reps/warranties (§ 8.2(a)(i)), Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.2(a)(iii)), and third-party claims (§ 8.2(a)(iv)). Limitations:         -             Basket ($87,500):         does             not         apply to environmental claims (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) — favorable.         -             Cap ($1,750,000):             does         apply to environmental claims — likely insufficient (Issue 6).         -             Survival (3 years):         applies to environmental reps and Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities — likely too short for groundwater contamination (Issue 6).         -             Sole remedy:         indemnification is the Buyer’s sole remedy, except for fraud/intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.2(b), 8.4(d)) — the fraud exception is significant given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations (Issue 7).  
437	        6.3 Representations (APA §§ 4.12, 4.13)  
438	      As detailed in Issue 7, the Seller’s environmental representations appear breached in multiple respects: § 4.12(a) (releases — Schedule 4.12(a)         “    None    ”         is inaccurate), § 4.12(b) (compliance — 2003 NOV), § 4.12(c) (no Environmental Notice — Schedule 4.12(c)         “    None    ”         is inaccurate; the 2003 NOV is an Environmental Notice), § 4.12(d) (completeness of document delivery — corporate records and consent order not produced), § 4.12(e) (no ICs/AULs — the GMZ may be an institutional control), and § 4.13/Schedule 4.13 (UST list incomplete — two USTs not disclosed).  
439	      
440	         7. CERCLA / AAI DEFENSE ANALYSIS  
441	      The Phase I ESA is intended to support the Buyer’s CERCLA defenses: the innocent landowner defense (§ 101(35)), the contiguous property owner defense (§ 107(q)), and the bona fide prospective purchaser (BFPP) defense (§ 107(r)).  
442	             BFPP defense (§ 107(r)):         Requires all appropriate inquiries before acquisition (facially met by the E1527-21 ESA, subject to the Issue 2 and Issue 13 concerns) plus continuing obligations after acquisition (reasonable steps, cooperation, compliance with institutional controls, etc.). The Buyer must be prepared to take reasonable steps regarding the identified contamination and to comply with any GMZ restrictions.  
443	             Contiguous property owner defense (§ 107(q)):         Directly relevant because the Property is threatened by contamination migrating from the adjacent Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility (PCE plume, Issue 2). To qualify, the Buyer must, among other things, not have caused or contributed to the contamination, conduct all appropriate inquiries, provide cooperation and assistance, comply with land-use restrictions, and take reasonable steps to prevent/reduce exposure.             The ESA’s failure to identify the PCE plume as a REC undermines the Buyer’s ability to demonstrate all appropriate inquiries and reasonable steps.         This should be corrected by a supplemented ESA and a Phase II that delineates PCE beneath the Property.  
444	             AAI adequacy:         The unresolved data gaps (Issue 13) and the missed RECs (Issues 2, 8, 9, 12) call into question whether the ESA, as written, constitutes         “    all appropriate inquiries.    ”         The Lender’s counsel will independently review for AAI compliance (Lender § 4).  
445	          Recommendation.         Supplement the ESA to identify all RECs (including the PCE plume); complete the Phase II; document all inquiries and data-gap efforts; and establish a continuing-obligations plan (reasonable steps, GMZ compliance, cooperation with the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP parties) to preserve the defenses.  
446	      
447	        8. CROSS-CUTTING OBSERVATIONS  
448	             The ESA under-classified the Property’s environmental condition.         At least three conditions that should be RECs were not classified as such: the off-site PCE plume (Issue 2), the two unregistered USTs (Issue 8), and the former drum storage area (Issue 9). One condition was misclassified (the 500-gal heating oil UST as an HREC, Issue 12). The Buyer and Lender should not rely on the ESA’s two-REC finding as complete.  
449	             The Seller’s due-diligence cooperation was incomplete.         Seller declined to produce 1961–1990 corporate records, could not produce the 2003 consent order or compliance certification, could not explain MW-1, and could not detail the former drum storage area. The 1988 spill was not reported to environmental databases. This pattern limits the Buyer’s ability to characterize risk and supports skepticism about the Seller’s environmental representations.  
450	             The indemnity package does not match the risk.         A $1,750,000 cap and 3-year survival period are mismatched against a contamination profile that includes 30+ years of TCE, 39 years of hexavalent chromium, an off-site PCE plume, and unaccounted-for USTs. The Lender independently requires ≥$5M insurance and possible escrow — signaling that the Seller’s package is, in the Lender’s view, insufficient standing alone.  
451	             The off-site PCE plume is the defining issue and was missed.         It triggers vapor intrusion, the contiguous property owner defense, GMZ land-use restrictions, and Lender decline risk — yet it was not classified as a REC.  
452	             Document-production quality control failed.         Three of eight documents are for a different property (Issue 1). This must be corrected before any party relies on the record.

455	      As explained in Issue 1, three documents in the production pertain to             2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414         (Montgomery County), owned by             Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.         — a different property, different seller, different price ($4,200,000), and different consultant (Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc.). These documents are reviewed here only because they were produced and contain their own issues;             they must not be applied to the Toledo acquisition.         If Cornerstone is separately pursuing the Dayton property, the following issues apply to that transaction.  
456	       Dayton Issue D1 — Tank 3 (4,000-gallon waste oil UST) closure never completed [CRITICAL for the Dayton transaction]  
457	          Description and evidence.         The Redland UST Closure Report (November 12, 2004) documents removal of three USTs at the Dayton property in September 2004. Tank 1 (10,000-gal diesel) and Tank 2 (10,000-gal diesel) were closed with confirmation sampling below BUSTR action levels (Tank 2 required over-excavation of 85 cubic yards; initial DRO up to 1,250 mg/kg, post-over-excavation max 280 mg/kg).             Tank 3 (4,000-gallon waste oil UST), however, was never closed.         Field observations during Tank 3 removal documented: moderate-to-heavy external corrosion;             one visible through-wall perforation (approximately 1.5 inches) on the tank bottom    ; approximately 4 inches of dark, viscous, oily residue; and             stained soil (dark black, strong petroleum/waste oil odor) at the excavation base and along the lower ~2 feet of the south and west sidewalls    . The seven confirmation soil samples for Tank 3 were collected but             analytical results were pending at the time of the report and were never received.         The report states:         “    A supplemental report will be submitted for Tank 3 upon receipt.    ”         An editor’s note confirms:         “        No supplemental report addressing Tank 3 analytical results was located in the seller’s project files, in the files of Redland Environmental Services, or in publicly available Ohio EPA records.    ”         The Tank 3 excavation was only             temporarily backfilled         (without compaction control) and paved over. The Terravista ESA confirms that closure letters were provided for Tank 1 and Tank 2 but             not for Tank 3         (the facility manager was         “    confident    ”         it was issued but could not locate it).  
458	          Consequence.         The waste oil UST had a through-wall perforation and visibly stained, odorous soil, but no confirmation analytical data, no supplemental report, and no closure letter exist. The closure was never completed. The 2011 VAP NFA letter for         “    Parcel A    ”         describes the area as the         “    former diesel underground storage tank area and associated petroleum-impacted soils    ”         — which may             not         encompass the waste-oil Tank 3 area. Waste oil (used crankcase oil, cutting oils, hydraulic fluids) may contain metals and other contaminants beyond petroleum. This is an unaddressed release for the Dayton property.  
459	          Recommendation (Dayton only).         Require a Phase II investigation of the former Tank 3 area (soil and groundwater sampling for petroleum hydrocarbons, metals, and waste-oil constituents); obtain or reconstruct the Tank 3 closure documentation; confirm whether the 2011 VAP NFA letter covers the Tank 3 area.  
460	        Dayton Issue D2 — REC-1 (western yard former ASTs) and REC-2 (Building 2 electroplating floor staining) [HIGH for Dayton]  
461	      The Terravista ESA identified two RECs for the Dayton property: (i) stressed/absent vegetation and darkened soil (~40 ft × 60 ft) in the western yard at the location of former Dayton Forge & Die above-ground storage tanks (1942 Sanborn         “    oil storage    ”         annotation); and (ii) floor staining (~15 ft × 20 ft) in Building 2 near the chemical drum storage area, where active chromium/nickel electroplating has occurred since 1987. Phase II investigation is recommended for both. (These findings apply to the Dayton property only.)  
462	        Dayton Issue D3 — HREC-2 (Central Valley Chemical Co.) likely misclassified [HIGH for Dayton]  
463	          Description and evidence.         The Terravista ESA classifies the former Central Valley Chemical Co. operations (Building 3, ~1975–1981, chemical blending of industrial cleaning solvents and degreasers including chlorinated solvents) as an HREC, relying on the 2011 VAP NFA letter. However, the ESA elsewhere states the NFA letter         “    covers a 2.3-acre area designated as Parcel A in the southern portion of the site    ”         and         “    does not address environmental conditions in areas of the Subject Property outside the Parcel A boundary.    ”         Building 3 (where Central Valley Chemical operated) is in the northeastern portion of the site,             outside         Parcel A. The ESA is internally inconsistent: it bases the HREC classification on a NFA letter that, by its own description, does not cover the Building 3 area. Additional evidence of a potential release at Building 3 includes: the 1971 aerial photograph showing ~100+ drums in outdoor storage on the north side of Building 3 (no secondary containment); a 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical (details not available — Ohio EPA file review pending); exterior staining (~8 ft × 12 ft) on the Building 3 loading dock; and floor cracking, patching, and ~3 inches of differential settlement inside Building 3.  
464	          Consequence.         Classifying Central Valley Chemical as an HREC implies the condition is resolved. But the NFA letter does not cover Building 3, and there is evidence of a potential chlorinated-solvent release (drum storage, enforcement action, staining, floor deterioration). This is likely a misclassification; the condition may warrant REC status and Phase II investigation (including vapor intrusion assessment for chlorinated solvents).  
465	          Recommendation (Dayton only).         Reclassify Central Valley Chemical as a REC (or at minimum a significant data gap); conduct a Phase II at Building 3 (soil, groundwater, soil gas for chlorinated solvents); obtain the 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement records (see D4).  
466	        Dayton Issue D4 — Ohio EPA file review not received; 1981 enforcement action unreviewed [MODERATE for Dayton]  
467	      The Terravista ESA requested Ohio EPA files on March 10, 2025, but had not received them as of the April 18, 2025 report date. The 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical Co. was therefore not reviewed. This is a data gap for the Dayton property.  
468	        Dayton Issue D5 — Partially buried 55-gallon drum (northwest boundary) not classified [MODERATE for Dayton]  
469	      A partially buried, significantly corroded 55-gallon drum with no visible label was observed near the northwest property boundary. The facility manager was unaware of its origin. The Terravista ESA noted it but did not classify it as a REC. A buried drum of unknown contents on the property warrants investigation and proper removal.  
470	        Dayton Issue D6 — Unlabeled older electrical transformer (Building 3) — potential PCB concern [MODERATE for Dayton]  
471	      One pad-mounted transformer adjacent to Building 3 had no PCB label (the other three transformers on site were labeled         “    Non-PCB    ”    ). The unlabeled unit appeared older with surface corrosion. No leaking was observed. The absence of a PCB label on an older transformer warrants verification of PCB status with the utility (Dayton Power & Grid Co.).  
472	        Dayton Issue D7 — ASTM E1527-13 (older standard) used instead of E1527-21 [MODERATE for Dayton]  
473	      The Terravista ESA was prepared to ASTM E1527-13, not the current E1527-21. While E1527-13 may still satisfy the AAI rule, the current standard is E1527-21. If the Dayton property is being acquired with lender financing or BFPP-defense reliance, confirm that E1527-13 is acceptable to the lender and sufficient for AAI; an update to E1527-21 may be warranted.  
474	        Dayton Issue D8 — GeoSearch RCRA waste codes and report-number discrepancy [LOW for Dayton]  
475	      The GeoSearch database summary lists RCRA waste codes for Valtec as D006 (cadmium), D007 (chromium), F001 (spent halogenated solvents used in degreasing), and F002 (spent halogenated solvents). The Terravista ESA, however, describes Valtec’s hazardous waste as         “    spent plating solutions, wastewater treatment sludge (F006), and miscellaneous solvent-contaminated rags and wipes    ”         — and does not mention F001/F002 halogenated solvents. The F001/F002 codes suggest historical chlorinated-solvent use/degreasing at the Dayton property that the ESA does not fully discuss. Additionally, the Terravista ESA references GeoSearch Report No. GS-2025-11847, while the GeoSearch summary lists Project/Order No. GS-2025-04817 and Inquiry No. INQ-OH-0328-7792 — a report-number discrepancy that should be reconciled.  
476	        Dayton Issue D9 — Non-scope items (ACM, LBP) [LOW for Dayton]  
477	      The Terravista ESA noted potential ACM (Buildings 1 and 2, constructed 1942 and 1955) and potential LBP (Buildings 1, 2, 3, and 5, all pre-1978) as non-scope items. Separate surveys are recommended before renovation/demolition.  
478	      
479	         10. PRIORITIZED RECOMMENDATIONS  
480	       Immediate (before the October 31, 2024 diligence deadline)  
481	              Segregate the Dayton documents         from the Toledo file and confirm with the deal team whether they were produced in error or pertain to a separate transaction (Issue 1).  
482	              Demand a supplemented/amended ESA         that (a) classifies the off-site PCE plume as a REC (Issue 2), (b) classifies the two unregistered USTs as RECs (Issue 8), (c) classifies the former drum storage area as a REC (Issue 9), and (d) reclassifies the 500-gal heating oil UST from HREC to REC (Issue 12). This is necessary to satisfy the Lender’s § 2 requirement.  
483	              Demand that Seller amend Schedules 4.12(a), 4.12(c), and 4.13         to disclose the 1988 spill, the 2003 NOV/consent order, the PCE plume/GMZ, and the two additional USTs (Issue 7).  
484	              Submit the Ohio EPA Central Office records request         for the 2003 consent order file (File No. NW-2003-0471) immediately, allowing 30–45 business days (Issue 11).  
485	              Negotiate a written extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period         beyond October 31, 2024, to allow Phase II cost estimates to inform the § 6.3(b) termination decision (Issue 14). If no extension is granted, evaluate by October 31 whether a termination notice is warranted.  
486	              Begin PLL insurance underwriting in parallel         — it is non-negotiable and on the critical path (Issue 5).  
487	        Before closing (December 15, 2024)  
488	              Commission a Phase II ESA         scoped to all RECs (TCE degreasing area and drum; hexavalent chromium electroplating room and sump; off-site PCE plume with on-Property groundwater wells; the two unregistered USTs via geophysical survey; the former drum storage area; and a vapor intrusion assessment), with the three-scenario remediation cost estimates the Lender requires (Issues 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 15).  
489	              Characterize and remove the abandoned waste-TCE drum         and stained soil behind the maintenance building (Issue 3).  
490	              Secure/repair monitoring well MW-1         and investigate its origin and any associated data (Issue 10).  
491	              Renegotiate the indemnity package    : increase the cap and/or create an environmental-specific carve-out; extend the survival period; require Seller to fund a portion of the PLL premium and/or escrow (Issue 6).  
492	              Establish the remediation escrow         (if most-likely costs exceed $250,000) and the remediation plan (if costs exceed $500,000), per Lender §§ 3.B.3–3.B.4 (Issue 5).  
493	              Confirm Lender decline-trigger exposure    : assess worst-case remediation cost vs. 50% of appraised value (§ 3.C(a)); confirm no active RCRA corrective action (§ 3.C(c)); and close all data gaps practicable (§ 3.C(d)) (Issues 5, 11, 13).  
494	              Commission ACM and LBP surveys         before any renovation/demolition (Issue 20).  
495	        Post-closing / continuing obligations  
496	              Implement a continuing-obligations plan         to preserve CERCLA defenses: take reasonable steps regarding identified contamination; comply with GMZ restrictions; cooperate with the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP parties and Ohio EPA; maintain the PLL insurance for the full 10-year term; comply with the Lender’s post-closing covenants (release notification, no UST activity without consent, annual certifications) (Issues 2, 5, 7).  
497	              Preserve all fraud/intentional misrepresentation arguments         given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations — the cap, basket, and survival period do not apply to fraud (Issues 6, 7).  
498	      
499	         11. LIMITATIONS  
500	      This memorandum is based solely on the eight documents listed in Section 2.1, which were provided for review. We did not independently verify the accuracy of the database reports, regulatory correspondence, or the Phase I ESA’s field observations, and we did not conduct an independent site reconnaissance or sampling. The severity ratings reflect our professional judgment based on the record as produced and are intended to assist the Buyer and its counsel in prioritizing due-diligence and negotiation actions; they are not a guarantee of any particular environmental outcome, remediation cost, or regulatory determination. The identification of conditions that         “    should have been classified as RECs    ”         reflects our review against ASTM E1527-21 and the Lender’s requirements; the environmental consultant of record is responsible for the ESA’s classifications, and any reclassification should be formally issued by a qualified Environmental Professional. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared in anticipation of litigation and in connection with the proposed transaction, and should not be disclosed to third parties without the consent of the Buyer and its counsel.  
501	      
502	          End of Memorandum.  
503

### FILE: environmental-issues-memo.docx
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / DUE DILIGENCE  
        ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES MEMORANDUM  
          Re:         Proposed Acquisition of 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611 (Lucas County Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, -002, -003)  
          Transaction:         Asset Purchase Agreement dated September 1, 2024 — Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. (Seller) and Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (Buyer); Purchase Price $8,750,000; Loan No. GLCB-2024-08173 (Great Lakes Capital Bank, $6,125,000)  
          Prepared for:         Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (Buyer/User) and Whitfield & Crane LLP (Buyer’s Counsel; Sarah Castellano, lead partner)  
          Re:         Review of Phase I ESA and supporting environmental documents; severity-rated issues  
          Date of this memorandum:         [Date of review]  
      
       1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY  
      This memorandum reports the findings of our review of the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (Phase I ESA) and supporting environmental documents produced in connection with Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC’s proposed acquisition of the approximately 14.7-acre former manufacturing/warehouse complex at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio (the         “    Property    ”    ) from Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. (    “    Seller    ”         or         “    Breckenridge    ”    ). The controlling transaction documents — the Asset Purchase Agreement (APA) dated September 1, 2024 and the Great Lakes Capital Bank (    “    Lender    ”    ) environmental due diligence requirements letter dated September 10, 2024 — both unambiguously identify the Toledo Property as the subject of the proposed acquisition.  
      We reviewed eight (8) documents.             Three of those eight documents do not pertain to the Property at all         — they concern a different property located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio (Montgomery County), owned by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. This is addressed as Issue 1 and is the single most important structural finding in the production: a portion of the environmental due-diligence record relates to a different transaction and must not be relied upon for the Toledo acquisition.  
      For the Toledo Property itself, the Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. Phase I ESA (dated October 25, 2024, ASTM E1527-21) identified two recognized environmental conditions (RECs): (i) former trichloroethylene (TCE) vapor degreasing operations (1961–1992) with an abandoned, leaking waste-TCE drum; and (ii) former hexavalent chromium/nickel electroplating operations (1965–2004) with visible staining and an in-ground sump containing liquid with a sheen. In our review, the ESA             under-classified the environmental condition of the Property in at least three material respects    : it failed to identify the off-site tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater plume migrating onto the Property from the adjacent former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility as a REC (contrary to the Lender’s express requirement); it failed to classify two unregistered, undocumented underground storage tanks (USTs) as RECs; and it failed to classify the former outdoor drum storage area (the location of a documented 2003 Ohio EPA Notice of Violation) as a REC.  
      The combined environmental profile — 30+ years of TCE degreasing, 39 years of hexavalent chromium electroplating with no hazardous waste manifests for any year before 2004, an off-site chlorinated-solvent plume in a state-designated groundwater management zone (GMZ), and two unaccounted-for USTs — triggers the Lender’s enhanced (    “    REC    ”    ) due-diligence requirements (Phase II ESA, ≥$5,000,000 pollution legal liability insurance, possible remediation escrow, possible remediation plan, and a vapor intrusion assessment) and raises serious questions about the adequacy of the Seller’s indemnity package (a $1,750,000 cap and a 3-year survival period) and the accuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations (Sections 4.12 and 4.13 of the APA).  
      We identified             thirty-four (34) distinct issues    , rated Critical (5), High (8), Moderate (10), Low (7), and Non-Issue/Informational (4) for the Toledo Property, plus an additional nine (9) issues within the misfiled Dayton-property documents (flagged separately so they are not erroneously applied to the Toledo acquisition). A prioritized recommendations section appears at Part 10.  
       Severity Rating Scale  
                         Rating  
          Meaning  
                CRITICAL  
          Condition that threatens transaction viability, risks Lender decline, or reflects a fundamental due-diligence/document-production failure; requires immediate action before closing.  
                HIGH  
          Recognized environmental condition or significant contractual/compliance exposure requiring Phase II investigation and likely to affect deal economics (price, indemnity, insurance, escrow); must be resolved before closing.  
                MODERATE  
          Condition warranting investigation, clarification, or contractual protection; data gaps, misclassifications, or secondary exposures; should be addressed before or at closing.  
                LOW  
          Minor issue, de minimis condition, or monitoring item; document and monitor.  
                NON-ISSUE  
          Apparent concern that analysis shows is not a problem (distractor); flagged for completeness.  
         
         2. SCOPE, DOCUMENTS REVIEWED, AND CONTROLLING FRAMEWORKS  
       2.1 Documents Reviewed  
                           #  
          Document  
          Property  
          Pertains to Acquisition?  
            1  
          Greenfield Phase I ESA (Oct 25, 2024; ASTM E1527-21; GEC-2024-0347)  
          4820 Lakeshore Industrial Dr., Toledo  
              Yes  
            2  
          Asset Purchase Agreement excerpt (dated Sept 1, 2024)  
          4820 Lakeshore Industrial Dr., Toledo  
              Yes  
            3  
          Great Lakes Capital Bank lender requirements letter (Sept 10, 2024)  
          4820 Lakeshore Industrial Dr., Toledo  
              Yes  
            4  
          Ohio EPA Northwest District Office response (Sept 28, 2024; DERR-NW-2024-RR-0438)  
          4820 Lakeshore Industrial Dr., Toledo  
              Yes

          EDR database summary (Sept 15, 2024; Inquiry No. 6742891)  
          4820 Lakeshore Industrial Dr., Toledo  
              Yes

          Terravista Phase I ESA (April 18, 2025; ASTM E1527-13; TEC-2025-0347)

           2.2 Controlling Frameworks  
      The deliverable is audited against the following frameworks, each of which is invoked by the source documents themselves:  
              ASTM E1527-21         — the Phase I ESA standard required by both the APA (§ 1.1 definition of         “    Phase I ESA    ”    ; § 6.3(a)) and the Lender letter (§ 2). The Greenfield ESA was prepared to this standard.  
              40 C.F.R. Part 312 (All Appropriate Inquiries / AAI rule)         — required to support CERCLA defenses (innocent landowner, contiguous property owner, bona fide prospective purchaser (BFPP)) per APA § 6.3(a) and Lender letter § 4.  
              The Asset Purchase Agreement         — §§ 4.12 (Environmental Matters), 4.13 (Underground Storage Tanks) and Schedule 4.13, 6.3 (Environmental Condition / termination right), 8.1–8.4 (indemnification, basket, cap, survival).  
              The Great Lakes Capital Bank lender requirements letter         — §§ 2 (Phase I requirements), 3.A–3.C (outcome scenarios and conditional requirements), 4 (AAI/CERCLA), 5 (delivery timeline), 6 (post-closing covenants).  
      
         3. ISSUES SUMMARY TABLE — TOLEDO PROPERTY (THE PROPOSED ACQUISITION)

          Lender         “    REC    ”         due-diligence requirements triggered (Phase II, PLL ≥$5M, possible escrow, possible remediation plan, vapor intrusion assessment)

          ESA §8.0; Ohio EPA; EDR  
              MODERATE  
            14  
          Timing mismatch: Environmental Due Diligence Period ends Oct 31, 2024; Phase II cost estimates not due until Nov 30, 2024; ESA dated Oct 25  
          APA §6.3; Lender §5

            15

        4. DETAILED ISSUE ANALYSIS — TOLEDO PROPERTY  
       CRITICAL ISSUES  
       Issue 1 — Three of eight produced documents pertain to a different property (Dayton/Valtec), not the Toledo acquisition  
          Severity: CRITICAL  
          Description and evidence.         The proposed acquisition is unambiguously the Toledo Property. The APA (dated September 1, 2024) defines the         “    Property    ”         as 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611 (Lucas County Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, -002, -003), sold by Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. to Cornerstone for $8,750,000, with a $6,125,000 loan from Great Lakes Capital Bank and a December 15, 2024 closing date. The Lender letter (September 10, 2024) confirms the same Property, loan number GLCB-2024-08173, and parties.  
      Three of the eight documents in the production do not concern this Property. They concern             2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414 (Montgomery County)    , owned by             Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.    , a different seller, a different city and county, a different purchase price ($4,200,000), and a different environmental consultant (Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc.):         - The Terravista Phase I ESA is dated             April 18, 2025         — after the Toledo closing date of December 15, 2024 — and is prepared to the older             ASTM E1527-13         standard, not the E1527-21 standard required by the APA and the Lender.         - The Redland Environmental Services UST Closure Report (November 12, 2004) is prepared         “    for Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.    ”         at         “    2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414.    ”         - The GeoSearch database search summary (March 28, 2025) lists the         “    Target Property    ”         as         “    2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414,    ”         Tax Parcel ID R72-04410-0025, prepared for Terravista.  
          Consequence.         Reliance on the Dayton documents for the Toledo acquisition would be inappropriate and potentially misleading: the two properties have different histories, different contaminants, different regulatory statuses, and different sellers. Conflating them could cause the Buyer to (a) credit a regulatory closure (the Dayton 2011 VAP NFA letter) that does not exist for the Toledo Property, (b) rely on an ESA prepared to the wrong ASTM standard, or (c) misallocate remediation responsibilities between two unrelated sellers. The presence of these documents also raises a document-production/quality-control concern: either the wrong files were produced for this transaction, or Cornerstone is pursuing a separate, parallel acquisition of the Dayton property that has not been disclosed in the Toledo transaction documents.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Confirm in writing with Cornerstone’s deal team whether the Dayton documents were produced in error or pertain to a separate transaction. (b) Segregate the Dayton documents from the Toledo due-diligence file and mark them         “    DO NOT RELY — DIFFERENT PROPERTY.    ”         (c) Do not cite the Dayton UST closure report, the Dayton VAP NFA letter, or the Dayton GeoSearch summary in any analysis of the Toledo Property. (d) The Dayton documents are nevertheless reviewed separately in Part 9 of this memorandum because they were produced and contain their own issues, but that review is expressly limited to the Dayton property.  
      
        Issue 2 — Off-site PCE groundwater plume (Lake Erie Dry Cleaning) migrating onto the Property; Property within a state-designated Groundwater Management Zone; not classified as a REC by the ESA  
          Severity: CRITICAL  
          Description and evidence.         The former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive is located immediately north of and hydraulically upgradient from the Property, approximately 120 feet from the northern property boundary. It is listed on the Ohio EPA Master Sites List under VAP Identification No. NW-0923 with a confirmed tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater contamination plume. The documented facts are:  
          A 2014 investigation identified PCE at             87 µg/L         at monitoring well             MW-7    , located approximately             120 feet north         of the Property boundary. The applicable Ohio generic numerical groundwater standard for PCE is             5 µg/L         (OAC § 3745-300-08, Table 1). The detected concentration             exceeds the standard by a factor of approximately 17.4    .  
          The documented plume migration direction is             south-southeast — toward the Property         — consistent with regional groundwater flow.  
          A Covenant Not to Sue was issued in 2018 under the Ohio VAP for the             dry cleaner parcel only    ; it             does not address off-site migration         of the PCE plume onto the Property.  
          An environmental covenant recorded under Ohio Rev. Code § 5301.82 restricts groundwater use and requires a sub-slab vapor mitigation system             on the dry cleaner parcel only    .  
          The Ohio EPA response (September 28, 2024) expressly states:         “        no investigation of PCE concentrations in groundwater beneath the Subject Property has been conducted, and the lateral and vertical extent of PCE plume migration onto or beneath the Subject Property has not been delineated.    ”  
          The Property is located within a             designated Groundwater Management Zone (GMZ)         established by Ohio EPA in connection with this plume. The GMZ         “    may impose restrictions on new groundwater withdrawals, new well installations, and certain land uses.    ”  
          The Greenfield ESA did not classify this condition as a REC.         The ESA describes the plume in detail in its database review (§ 5.1.1) and EDR appendix narrative, but the Findings section (§ 9.0) lists only REC-1 (TCE) and REC-2 (electroplating). The PCE plume appears in neither the REC, CREC, HREC, nor de minimis categories. This is a direct failure to comply with the Lender’s express instruction:         “    The Phase I ESA must evaluate whether any off-site contamination, including groundwater plumes, may be migrating toward or impacting the subject Property,             and any such conditions must be identified as recognized environmental conditions    ”         (Lender letter § 2).  
          Consequence.         This is, in our judgment, the most significant environmental condition affecting the Property, and it was not carried forward as a REC. The consequences are multiple:  
              Lender compliance.         The ESA, as written, does not satisfy the Lender’s § 2 requirement. The Lender’s environmental counsel (Whitfield & Crane LLP) is independently reviewing the ESA for AAI compliance and may reject it or require supplementation.  
              Vapor intrusion.         PCE is a volatile organic compound (VOC). A chlorinated-solvent plume beneath an occupied or occupiable structure presents a vapor intrusion risk. The Property has been used for warehousing since December 2019; warehouse workers and future occupants may be exposed. The Lender’s § 3.B.5 vapor intrusion assessment is triggered.  
              CERCLA contiguous property owner defense.         The Property is receiving (or is threatened with receiving) contamination from an adjacent property. The contiguous property owner defense under CERCLA § 107(q), 42 U.S.C. § 9607(q), requires, among other things, that the owner conducted all appropriate inquiries, is not the source of the contamination, provides cooperation and assistance to parties conducting cleanup, and takes reasonable steps to prevent or reduce exposure. The ESA’s failure to identify the plume as a REC undermines the Buyer’s ability to demonstrate that it conducted all appropriate inquiries and took reasonable steps. The GMZ may also impose land-use restrictions with which the Buyer must comply as a continuing obligation.  
              Regulatory/land-use restrictions.         The GMZ may restrict groundwater withdrawals, well installations, and certain land uses on the Property — a potential institutional control that affects redevelopment (the Buyer’s planned use is warehousing/light industrial).  
              Remediation cost exposure.         If PCE has migrated beneath the Property, the Buyer could face investigation, monitoring, and possible remediation costs, and could be drawn into the ongoing Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP matter.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Require the environmental consultant to issue a supplemented or amended ESA that expressly evaluates the off-site PCE plume and classifies it as a REC (or, at minimum, a CREC/vapor intrusion condition) consistent with the Lender’s requirement. (b) Commission a Phase II ESA that includes groundwater monitoring wells on the Property (downgradient of MW-7) to delineate whether PCE has migrated beneath the Property, plus a vapor intrusion assessment (sub-slab soil gas and/or indoor air) for occupied structures. (c) Request the complete Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP file (VAP ID NW-0923) and the GMZ establishment documentation from Ohio EPA to understand the full extent and any use restrictions. (d) Evaluate whether the GMZ constitutes an         “    institutional control    ”         or         “    activity and use limitation    ”         that must be disclosed and that may breach Seller’s representation in APA § 4.12(e). (e) Confirm the Buyer’s eligibility for, and document the conditions of, the contiguous property owner defense.  
      
        Issue 3 — REC-1: Former TCE vapor degreasing operations (1961–1992) and abandoned, leaking waste-TCE drum  
          Severity: CRITICAL  
          Description and evidence.         Trichloroethylene (TCE) was used as a vapor degreasing solvent in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building from 1961 through approximately 1992 — a period of more than 30 years. The evidence of a release includes:  
          A photoionization detector (PID) reading of             42.3 ppm         was recorded at a crack in the floor slab of the former degreasing area. Background PID readings elsewhere in the building were generally             less than 0.5 ppm    . The 42.3 ppm reading is indicative of VOC vapors emanating from beneath the slab.  
          Visible dark staining penetrates the concrete floor across an approximately 4,000-square-foot area; interior walls show staining up to approximately three feet above floor level.  
              No closure documentation, decommissioning records, or soil/groundwater confirmation sampling         were located for the degreasing area following the reported cessation of TCE use in 1992.  
          An abandoned             55-gallon drum labeled                 “        WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE        ”         was observed on the ground surface behind the maintenance/utility building. The drum is approximately half full, with a rusted bottom and evidence of past leakage. Stained soil (approximately 3 ft × 3 ft, dark and oily, vegetation absent) was observed beneath the drum. Seller’s principal stated the drum had been         “    there for years    ”         and was         “    probably from the early ’90s.    ”  
          A             March 14, 1988 chemical spill         involving an         “    unknown quantity of degreasing solvent    ”         from an overturned drum was documented in City of Toledo Fire Department records. Cleanup was performed by Breckenridge employees; no follow-up inspection was conducted. (Notably, this spill does             not         appear in the federal ERNS database or the Ohio EPA Spills database — see Issue 13.)  
          Consequence.         TCE is a chlorinated solvent and a known human carcinogen. It is highly mobile in groundwater and persists in the environment. The combination of 30+ years of use, an elevated PID reading at a floor crack, undocumented closure, a leaking waste drum with stained soil, and a documented 1988 spill strongly indicates a release of chlorinated solvents to soil and likely to groundwater in the northwest portion of the main building and behind the maintenance building. Vapor intrusion into occupied warehouse space is a significant concern. Remediation of chlorinated solvent contamination (soil and groundwater) is typically expensive and time-consuming, and may exceed the Seller’s $1,750,000 indemnity cap (Issue 6).  
          Recommendation.         (a) Phase II ESA: advance soil borings through the floor slab in the former degreasing area and behind the maintenance building; install groundwater monitoring wells; analyze for VOCs including TCE and its degradation products (cis-1,2-dichloroethylene, vinyl chloride). (b) Immediately characterize and properly dispose of the abandoned waste-TCE drum and the stained soil beneath it; do not close until the drum is removed. (c) Conduct a vapor intrusion assessment for the former degreasing area and any occupied structures, per Lender § 3.B.5. (d) Preserve the 1988 Fire Department spill record as evidence relevant to Seller’s § 4.12(a) representation.  
      
        Issue 4 — REC-2: Former hexavalent chromium/nickel electroplating operations (1965–2004)  
          Severity: CRITICAL  
          Description and evidence.         Electroplating operations using hexavalent chromium and nickel plating solutions were conducted from 1965 through 2004 in the south wing of the main manufacturing building — approximately 39 years. The evidence includes:  
          Prominent             blue-green staining         on the concrete floor and lower walls (to approximately four feet), consistent with chromium residues; the concrete shows pitting, etching, and chemical degradation from prolonged exposure to acidic/alkaline plating solutions.  
          An             in-ground concrete sump         (approximately 3 ft × 3 ft × 3 ft deep) in the center of the former electroplating room contains approximately             four inches of liquid with a visible iridescent sheen         and a greenish tint. The source and composition were not determined (no sampling was performed, consistent with Phase I scope).  
              Hazardous waste disposal documentation exists only for the final year of operations (2004):         six manifests documenting disposal of             14,200 pounds         of waste under RCRA codes             D007 (chromium)         and             F006 (electroplating sludge)    , transported by Tri-County Environmental Hauling Inc.         No manifests, biennial reports, or disposal records exist for any of the preceding 39 years (1965–2003).         The Ohio EPA response confirms this:         “    Ohio EPA has no hazardous waste manifests, biennial reports, or annual hazardous waste generation reports on file for this facility for any reporting year other than 2004.    ”  
          A             2003 Ohio EPA compliance inspection         (July 17, 2003) documented improper storage of hazardous waste — specifically,         “    multiple rusted and visibly leaking 55-gallon drums in an outdoor storage area located on the east side    ”         containing electroplating sludge and spent chromium-bearing wastewater, with staining on the ground beneath and around the drums. A Notice of Violation (File No. NW-2003-0471) was issued August 12, 2003 and resolved through a Director’s Final Findings and Orders (consent order) (see Issues 9 and 11).  
          Consequence.         Hexavalent chromium is a potent toxin and known carcinogen; nickel compounds are also regulated. The 39-year operating history, visible chromium residues, liquid waste with sheen in the sump, the near-total absence of hazardous waste disposal documentation (raising the question of how 39 years of chromium-bearing waste was managed), and the documented 2003 regulatory violation collectively indicate a likely release of chromium and related metals to the subsurface beneath and around the former electroplating room. The absence of disposal records for 1965–2003 is itself a significant compliance and liability concern: it suggests either undocumented on-site disposal, undocumented off-site shipment, or recordkeeping failures, any of which could expose the Buyer to successor liability or regulatory scrutiny. Remediation of metals-contaminated soil and groundwater, plus sump liquid characterization and disposal, is likely to be costly.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Phase II ESA: characterize the sump liquid (metals, VOCs, pH, other parameters); advance soil borings through and beneath the floor slab in the former electroplating room; install groundwater monitoring wells; analyze for total and hexavalent chromium, nickel, and other plating-related metals. (b) Investigate the 1965–2003 waste management history; request any remaining corporate records (see Issue 13) and interview former employees if locatable. (c) Obtain and review the 2003 consent order and compliance certification (see Issue 11). (d) Budget for sump liquid characterization and disposal as an immediate post-closing (or pre-closing) action.  
      
         HIGH ISSUES  
       Issue 5 — Lender         “    REC    ”         due-diligence requirements triggered (Phase II, PLL insurance ≥$5M, possible escrow, possible remediation plan, vapor intrusion assessment)  
          Severity: HIGH  
          Description and evidence.         The Lender letter § 3.B provides that if the Phase I ESA identifies one or more RECs, the Lender         “    will             not         fund the Loan based solely on the Phase I ESA.    ”         The Borrower must then satisfy             all         of the following before closing:         1.             Phase II ESA         (ASTM E1903-19) with quantified remediation cost estimates (best-case, most-likely, worst-case) prepared by a licensed professional engineer.         2.             Pollution Legal Liability (PLL) insurance         from a carrier rated A- or better, with limits of not less than the greater of (x) the worst-case remediation cost estimate or (y)             $5,000,000    , term of not less than             10 years    , with the Bank as additional insured and loss payee. The Lender states this is         “        non-negotiable    ”         and that it         “    will not rely solely on seller indemnification provisions.    ”         3.             Remediation cost escrow         at Great Lakes Capital Bank equal to             125% of the most-likely remediation cost estimate    , if that estimate exceeds             $250,000    .         4.             Remediation plan         (by a licensed environmental professional, submitted to/approved by Ohio EPA where applicable, completed within 36 months of closing), if estimated costs exceed             $500,000         under either the most-likely or worst-case scenario.         5.             Vapor intrusion assessment         (per Ohio EPA/U.S. EPA guidance) and, if warranted, a vapor mitigation plan implemented prior to or concurrently with closing, if VOCs are identified in soil or groundwater at or beneath the Property.  
      The Greenfield ESA identified two RECs (TCE and hexavalent chromium). Our review identifies at least three additional conditions that should have been classified as RECs (the off-site PCE plume (Issue 2), two unregistered USTs (Issue 8), and the former drum storage area (Issue 9)). The Lender’s § 3.B requirements are therefore triggered, and the scope of the required Phase II and cost estimates is broader than the ESA’s two-REC findings would suggest.  
          Consequence.         The Borrower faces substantial additional cost and time before closing: a Phase II ESA with quantified cost estimates; a ≥$5,000,000, 10-year PLL policy (premiums can be significant); a possible escrow at 125% of most-likely remediation costs; a possible Ohio-EPA-approved remediation plan; and a vapor intrusion assessment/mitigation plan. The Lender also reserves the right (§ 3.C) to decline the loan if the worst-case remediation cost exceeds 50% of appraised value, if the Property is on the NPL, if there is an active RCRA corrective action order, or if unresolved data gaps prevent adequate risk characterization (see Issue 13). The 36-month remediation completion deadline (if triggered) and the 10-year insurance term extend the Buyer’s obligations well beyond the 3-year APA survival period (Issue 6).  
          Recommendation.         (a) Immediately commission a Phase II ESA scoped to all RECs (including the off-site PCE plume, the two unregistered USTs, and the drum storage area, in addition to the ESA’s two RECs), with the three-scenario cost estimates the Lender requires. (b) Begin PLL insurance underwriting in parallel — it is non-negotiable and on the critical path. (c) Model the escrow and remediation-plan triggers against the cost estimates. (d) Confirm the Lender’s appraisal and the 50%-of-appraised-value decline threshold (§ 3.C(a)) to assess decline risk. (e) Build the Phase II, insurance, escrow, and remediation costs into the deal economics and renegotiate price/indemnity accordingly.  
      
        Issue 6 — Seller indemnity package likely inadequate: $1,750,000 cap and 3-year survival period vs. likely remediation scope  
          Severity: HIGH  
          Description and evidence.         The APA indemnification provisions (Article VIII) provide:         -             Basket:         $87,500 (1% of purchase price) — a true deductible that converts to first-dollar coverage once exceeded. The basket does             not         apply to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) or to covenant breaches or third-party claims (§ 8.2(a)(ii), (iv)) — favorable to Buyer.         -             Cap:         Seller’s aggregate liability under Article VIII             shall not exceed $1,750,000         (20% of the purchase price). This cap             does apply         to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.4(b)).         -             Survival:         The environmental representations (§§ 4.12, 4.13) and the Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities indemnity (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) survive for             three (3) years         after closing (§ 8.4(c)). Claims must be noticed before expiration.         -             Exceptions:         The basket, cap, and survival period do             not         apply to fraud or intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.4(d)).         -             Sole remedy:         Indemnification is the Buyer’s sole and exclusive remedy, except for fraud/intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.2(b)).  
      The Lender letter expressly warns (§ 3.B.2) that seller indemnification provisions         “    are typically subject to caps, baskets, deductibles, and survival periods that may be insufficient to cover full remediation costs over the duration of the Bank’s exposure,    ”         and for that reason requires environmental insurance as a non-negotiable supplement.  
          Consequence.         The combined remediation scope for this Property — 30+ years of TCE degreasing, 39 years of hexavalent chromium electroplating, an off-site PCE plume, two unregistered USTs, and a former drum storage area — can readily exceed $1,750,000. Chlorinated solvent (TCE/PCE) groundwater remediation alone frequently runs into the millions. If remediation costs exceed the cap, the Buyer bears the excess with no recourse to Seller (absent fraud). The 3-year survival period is also short relative to the timeframe over which groundwater contamination is typically discovered and addressed; contamination that manifests or is discovered after 3 years is barred. The Lender’s required 10-year PLL insurance term and 36-month remediation deadline both extend well beyond the 3-year survival window, meaning the Buyer (and its insurer) will be bearing risk that Seller no longer covers.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Negotiate an increase to the indemnity cap and/or a separate, higher environmental-specific cap or carve-out for Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (the basket already does not apply to environmental claims — push for the cap not to apply, or to apply at a higher figure, to environmental claims). (b) Negotiate a longer survival period for environmental representations and Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (e.g., the statutory limitations period for CERCLA, or a minimum of 5–10 years). (c) Require Seller to fund a portion of the PLL insurance premium and/or the remediation escrow as a condition of closing. (d) Preserve all fraud/intentional misrepresentation arguments given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations (Issue 7) — the cap, basket, and survival period do not apply to fraud. (e) Confirm that the Lender’s required PLL insurance and escrow are in place to backstop the gap above the cap.  
      
        Issue 7 — Seller environmental representations (APA §§ 4.12, 4.13) likely breached by the record  
          Severity: HIGH  
          Description and evidence.         The Seller’s environmental representations and warranties, given as of the Effective Date (September 1, 2024) and as of the Closing Date, appear to be inaccurate in several respects based on the record:  
             § 4.12(a) —                 “        no Release of any Hazardous Substances at, on, under, or from the Property        ”                 (Schedule 4.12(a):                 “        None        ”        ).         The record documents multiple releases: the 1988 TCE/degreasing-solvent spill (Toledo Fire Department); the abandoned, leaking waste-TCE drum with stained soil; the 2003 Ohio EPA NOV documenting         “    rusted and visibly leaking 55-gallon drums    ”         with         “    staining on the ground surface beneath and around the drums    ”    ; and the off-site PCE plume migrating toward/onto the Property. Schedule 4.12(a) (    “    None    ”    ) appears inaccurate.  
             § 4.12(b) —                 “        material compliance with all applicable Environmental Laws.        ”         The 2003 Ohio EPA compliance inspection documented three violations (improper hazardous waste storage in leaking drums; failure to label containers with accumulation dates; failure to maintain hazardous waste determination records), resulting in a NOV and a consent order (Director’s Final Findings and Orders). This is a documented material compliance failure.  
             § 4.12(c) —                 “        no Environmental Notice        ”                 (Schedule 4.12(c):                 “        None        ”        ).         The 2003 NOV (File No. NW-2003-0471) is, by the APA’s own definition, an         “    Environmental Notice    ”         (a         “    notice of violation … from any Governmental Authority relating to any alleged or actual violation of Environmental Law    ”    ). Schedule 4.12(c) (    “    None    ”    ) appears inaccurate.  
             § 4.12(d) —                 “        delivered … true, correct, and complete copies of all environmental reports.        ”         Seller’s principal declined to produce corporate records from 1961–1990 (ESA § 7.1, Data Gap No. 2); the 2003 consent order and compliance certification were not produced (archived); and no records were produced for monitoring well MW-1 (Issue 10). The completeness of Seller’s document production is questionable.  
             § 4.12(e) —                 “        no environmental liens, deed restrictions, engineering controls, institutional controls, activity and use limitations, or environmental covenants.        ”         The Property is within a state-designated Groundwater Management Zone (GMZ) established by Ohio EPA in connection with the PCE plume (Ohio EPA response § 4). The GMZ         “    may impose restrictions on new groundwater withdrawals, new well installations, and certain land uses    ”         — potentially an institutional control/activity and use limitation. (The environmental covenant on the adjacent dry cleaner parcel at 4900 Lakeshore does not encumber the Property, but the GMZ may.)  
             § 4.13(a) and Schedule 4.13 —                 “        true, correct, and complete list of all Underground Storage Tanks.        ”         Schedule 4.13 lists only the active 10,000-gallon diesel UST (Facility ID 48-22-10-0087) and states:         “        No other Underground Storage Tanks are or have been located on the Property.    ”         The historical Sanborn maps document two additional USTs: a             2,000-gallon waste solvent UST         (1987 Sanborn) and a             500-gallon heating oil UST         (1948/1955 Sanborn, Halstead farmhouse). Ohio EPA/BUSTR confirms no registration or closure records exist for either (Issue 8). Schedule 4.13 is therefore incomplete and the representation is inaccurate.  
             § 4.13(b) —                 “        no Releases from any Underground Storage Tank.        ”         Given the unknown status of the 2,000-gal waste solvent and 500-gal heating oil USTs (no closure records, possible remaining in place), this representation cannot be verified and may be inaccurate.

        Issue 8 — Two unregistered, undocumented underground storage tanks not classified as RECs (missed RECs)  
          Severity: HIGH  
          Description and evidence.         Two USTs documented on historical Sanborn fire insurance maps are not registered with Ohio BUSTR, have no closure or removal records, and were not classified as RECs by the Greenfield ESA:  
             2,000-gallon waste solvent UST         — depicted on the 1987 Sanborn map on the east side of the Property, between the main building and the eastern property boundary, in the general vicinity of the former outdoor drum storage area (Issue 9). Ohio EPA/BUSTR confirms:         “    no registration filings, installation permits, closure notifications, tank removal reports, site assessment results, or any other documentation exists in BUSTR’s records.    ”         The EDR summary states the tank’s status is         “    UNKNOWN — No registration, no closure records.             Tank may remain in place.    ”  
             500-gallon heating oil UST         — depicted on the 1948 and 1955 Sanborn maps serving the Halstead family farmhouse in the northwest portion of the Property (farmhouse demolished 1961). Ohio EPA/BUSTR confirms no records exist. The EDR summary states:         “    UNKNOWN — Pre-1961 farmhouse demolished. No records of UST removal.             Tank may remain in place beneath current pavement/fill.    ”  
      The Greenfield ESA mentions both tanks in its historical review (§ 5.2.1) and site reconnaissance (§ 6.2.2) but does not classify either as a REC. The 500-gallon heating oil UST is classified as an HREC (see Issue 12 — a misclassification).  
          Consequence.         An unregistered, undocumented UST that         “    may remain in place    ”         is a textbook recognized environmental condition: it is a potential source of a release (past, ongoing, or future) of a hazardous substance/petroleum product, with no evidence that any release has been addressed. The 2,000-gallon             waste solvent         UST is especially significant:         “    waste solvent    ”         at a facility that used TCE for degreasing strongly suggests the tank held chlorinated solvents, which are VOCs and carcinogens — directly relevant to the vapor intrusion risk (Issue 15) and the TCE contamination (Issue 3). A UST of unknown integrity that may still contain waste solvent is a material threat of a future release. The failure to classify these as RECs understates the environmental condition of the Property and fails the Lender’s requirement that all RECs be identified.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Require the ESA to be supplemented to classify both USTs as RECs. (b) Phase II ESA: conduct a geophysical survey (ground-penetrating radar/electromagnetic) to locate the 2,000-gal waste solvent UST and the 500-gal heating oil UST; if located, advance soil borings and install groundwater monitoring wells; analyze for VOCs (solvent UST) and petroleum hydrocarbons (heating oil UST). An approximately 40 ft × 60 ft area on the east side of the Property, delineated by deteriorating concrete curbing, exhibits stressed (yellowed, sparse) vegetation relative to surrounding areas.         Issue 12 — 500-gallon Halstead farmhouse heating oil UST misclassified as an HREC  
          Severity: MODERATE  
          Description and evidence.         The Greenfield ESA classifies the 500-gallon heating oil UST (Halstead farmhouse, demolished 1961) as a Historical Recognized Environmental Condition (HREC No. 1). The ESA reasons that the tank         “    was likely removed when the farmhouse was demolished in 1961    ”         and that         “    no surface evidence of a UST … was observed,    ”         so the condition         “    does not present a current risk.    ”  
      This classification is questionable. Under ASTM E1527-21, an HREC is         “    a past release of any hazardous substances or petroleum products that has occurred in connection with the property and             has been addressed to the satisfaction of the applicable regulatory authority         or meets unrestricted use criteria … without subjecting the property to any required controls.    ”         Here, there is             no evidence         that the tank was removed, that any release was addressed, or that any regulatory authority reviewed or approved anything. Ohio EPA/BUSTR confirms no closure or removal records exist (Issue 8). The EDR summary states the tank         “    may remain in place beneath current pavement/fill.    ”         There is no documented regulatory closure.  
          Consequence.         An HREC signals to a reader (and to the Lender) that a past condition has been resolved and requires no further action. Classifying an undocumented, possibly-still-in-place UST as an HREC understates the condition and may cause the Buyer and Lender to forgo investigation of a potential release. This is a misclassification that should be corrected to a REC (or, at minimum, a significant data gap requiring investigation).  
          Recommendation.         (a) Require the ESA to reclassify the 500-gal heating oil UST from an HREC to a REC (consistent with Issue 8). (b) Include it in the Phase II geophysical survey and soil/groundwater investigation. (c) Confirm that the Lender is notified of the reclassification.
