          Recommendation.         (a) Make reasonable efforts to close each gap before the diligence deadline: request the consent order (Issue 11); request MW-1 records (Issue 10); press Seller for 1961–1990 corporate records; request Ohio EPA’s full facility file. (b) Document all gaps and the efforts made to close them, as the Lender requires (§ 2). (c) If gaps remain, disclose them to the Lender and assess § 3.C(d) risk. (d) Treat the 1988-spill/ERNS discrepancy as evidence relevant to Seller’s § 4.12(a) and (b) representations.  
      
        Issue 14 — Timing mismatch between the diligence/termination deadline and the Phase II/cost-estimate deadline  
          Severity: MODERATE  
          Description and evidence.         The APA establishes an             Environmental Due Diligence Period ending at 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time on October 31, 2024         (60 days after the September 1, 2024 Effective Date). Under § 6.3(b), if the Phase I ESA identifies RECs that         “    could result in Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000,    ”         the Buyer may terminate and recover its $437,500 earnest money deposit             only by written notice delivered on or before October 31, 2024    . If the Buyer fails to terminate by that date, the right is             automatically waived         (§ 6.3(c)) — though indemnification rights under Article VIII are preserved.  
      The Greenfield ESA is dated             October 25, 2024         — only             six days         before the termination deadline. The Lender’s Phase II ESA (with quantified cost estimates) is not due until             November 30, 2024         (15 days before the December 15, 2024 closing; Lender § 5). Thus, the Buyer must decide whether to terminate based on the Phase I ESA alone,             without         the quantified remediation cost estimates that the Lender requires and that the § 6.3(b) standard (    “    Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000    ”    ) implicitly contemplates.  
          Consequence.         The Buyer faces a Hobson’s choice: (a) terminate by October 31 based on qualitative Phase I findings (and forfeit the deal), or (b) waive the termination right and proceed, bearing the risk that Phase II cost estimates (available only around November 30) later reveal remediation costs far exceeding $500,000 (or the $1,750,000 indemnity cap). Given the RECs identified (TCE, hexavalent chromium, and the additional RECs we identify — PCE plume, two USTs, drum storage area), remediation costs plausibly exceed $500,000, so a § 6.3(b) termination notice would likely be defensible — but the Buyer would be terminating without cost data. The six-day window is also impractical for legal review of the ESA.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Negotiate an extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period (in writing, signed by both parties — § 6.3 requires a written amendment) to allow the Phase II cost estimates to inform the termination decision; tie the extension to the Lender’s November 30 Phase II deadline. (b) If an extension is not granted, evaluate by October 31 whether the qualitative Phase I findings (as supplemented by our additional REC findings) support a § 6.3(b) termination notice, and preserve the right. (c) Note that even if the termination right is waived, Article VIII indemnification (subject to cap/survival) and the fraud exception survive (§ 6.3(c); § 8.4(d)).  
      
        Issue 15 — Vapor intrusion risk (TCE, PCE, waste-solvent UST) with non-functional ventilation  
          Severity: MODERATE  
          Description and evidence.         Three sources of volatile organic compounds are present at or beneath the Property: (a) the former TCE degreasing area (REC-1, with a 42.3 ppm PID reading at a floor crack); (b) the off-site PCE plume migrating toward/onto the Property (Issue 2); and (c) the possible 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST (Issue 8), which, if it held chlorinated solvents, is an additional VOC source. The ESA notes that the ventilation systems in the former degreasing area and the former electroplating room are             non-functional         (disconnected ductwork, sealed exhaust ports). The Property has been used for warehousing since December 2019, meaning workers occupy the building where vapor intrusion could occur.  
          Consequence.         Chlorinated solvents (TCE, PCE) are VOCs that migrate from contaminated soil/groundwater into indoor air through vapor intrusion, posing inhalation exposure risks to occupants. The Lender requires a vapor intrusion assessment (§ 3.B.5) and, if warranted, a vapor mitigation plan implemented prior to or concurrently with closing. Vapor intrusion can also create construction-tort and worker-safety exposure for the Buyer as the post-closing owner/occupant.  
          Recommendation.         (a) Include a vapor intrusion assessment (sub-slab soil gas and/or indoor air sampling) in the Phase II ESA, focused on the former degreasing area, the former electroplating room, and any occupied warehouse space overlying the PCE plume footprint. (b) If vapor intrusion risks are confirmed, implement a vapor mitigation plan (e.g., sub-slab depressurization) before or at closing, as the Lender requires. (c) Address the non-functional ventilation systems as part of redevelopment planning.

        6. PURCHASE AGREEMENT ANALYSIS  
       6.1 Termination right (APA § 6.3(b))  
      The Buyer may terminate and recover its $437,500 earnest money deposit if the Phase I ESA identifies RECs that         “    could result in Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000,    ”         by written notice on or before             October 31, 2024    . The right is automatically waived if not exercised (§ 6.3(c)); indemnification rights under Article VIII are preserved regardless.  
          Analysis.         The ESA’s two RECs (TCE, hexavalent chromium), plus the additional RECs we identify (PCE plume, two USTs, drum storage area), readily support a conclusion that remediation costs         “    could exceed $500,000.    ”         A § 6.3(b) termination notice would be defensible. However, the Buyer would be terminating without the Phase II cost estimates (not due until November 30), and the six-day window between the ESA date (October 25) and the deadline (October 31) is impractical (Issue 14). The Buyer should seek a written extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period.  
        6.2 Indemnification (APA §§ 8.2, 8.4)  
      Seller indemnifies for breach of reps/warranties (§ 8.2(a)(i)), Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.2(a)(iii)), and third-party claims (§ 8.2(a)(iv)). Limitations:         -             Basket ($87,500):         does             not         apply to environmental claims (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) — favorable.         -             Cap ($1,750,000):             does         apply to environmental claims — likely insufficient (Issue 6).         -             Survival (3 years):         applies to environmental reps and Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities — likely too short for groundwater contamination (Issue 6).         -             Sole remedy:         indemnification is the Buyer’s sole remedy, except for fraud/intentional misrepresentation (§ 8.2(b), 8.4(d)) — the fraud exception is significant given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations (Issue 7).  
        6.3 Representations (APA §§ 4.12, 4.13)  
      As detailed in Issue 7, the Seller’s environmental representations appear breached in multiple respects: § 4.12(a) (releases — Schedule 4.12(a)         “    None    ”         is inaccurate), § 4.12(b) (compliance — 2003 NOV), § 4.12(c) (no Environmental Notice — Schedule 4.12(c)         “    None    ”         is inaccurate; the 2003 NOV is an Environmental Notice), § 4.12(d) (completeness of document delivery — corporate records and consent order not produced), § 4.12(e) (no ICs/AULs — the GMZ may be an institutional control), and § 4.13/Schedule 4.13 (UST list incomplete — two USTs not disclosed).  
      
         7. CERCLA / AAI DEFENSE ANALYSIS  
      The Phase I ESA is intended to support the Buyer’s CERCLA defenses: the innocent landowner defense (§ 101(35)), the contiguous property owner defense (§ 107(q)), and the bona fide prospective purchaser (BFPP) defense (§ 107(r)).  
             BFPP defense (§ 107(r)):         Requires all appropriate inquiries before acquisition (facially met by the E1527-21 ESA, subject to the Issue 2 and Issue 13 concerns) plus continuing obligations after acquisition (reasonable steps, cooperation, compliance with institutional controls, etc.). The Buyer must be prepared to take reasonable steps regarding the identified contamination and to comply with any GMZ restrictions.  
             Contiguous property owner defense (§ 107(q)):         Directly relevant because the Property is threatened by contamination migrating from the adjacent Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility (PCE plume, Issue 2). To qualify, the Buyer must, among other things, not have caused or contributed to the contamination, conduct all appropriate inquiries, provide cooperation and assistance, comply with land-use restrictions, and take reasonable steps to prevent/reduce exposure.             The ESA’s failure to identify the PCE plume as a REC undermines the Buyer’s ability to demonstrate all appropriate inquiries and reasonable steps.         This should be corrected by a supplemented ESA and a Phase II that delineates PCE beneath the Property.  
             AAI adequacy:         The unresolved data gaps (Issue 13) and the missed RECs (Issues 2, 8, 9, 12) call into question whether the ESA, as written, constitutes         “    all appropriate inquiries.    ”         The Lender’s counsel will independently review for AAI compliance (Lender § 4).  
          Recommendation.         Supplement the ESA to identify all RECs (including the PCE plume); complete the Phase II; document all inquiries and data-gap efforts; and establish a continuing-obligations plan (reasonable steps, GMZ compliance, cooperation with the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP parties) to preserve the defenses.  
      
        8. CROSS-CUTTING OBSERVATIONS  
             The ESA under-classified the Property’s environmental condition.         At least three conditions that should be RECs were not classified as such: the off-site PCE plume (Issue 2), the two unregistered USTs (Issue 8), and the former drum storage area (Issue 9). One condition was misclassified (the 500-gal heating oil UST as an HREC, Issue 12). The Buyer and Lender should not rely on the ESA’s two-REC finding as complete.  
             The Seller’s due-diligence cooperation was incomplete.         Seller declined to produce 1961–1990 corporate records, could not produce the 2003 consent order or compliance certification, could not explain MW-1, and could not detail the former drum storage area. The 1988 spill was not reported to environmental databases. This pattern limits the Buyer’s ability to characterize risk and supports skepticism about the Seller’s environmental representations.  
             The indemnity package does not match the risk.         A $1,750,000 cap and 3-year survival period are mismatched against a contamination profile that includes 30+ years of TCE, 39 years of hexavalent chromium, an off-site PCE plume, and unaccounted-for USTs. The Lender independently requires ≥$5M insurance and possible escrow — signaling that the Seller’s package is, in the Lender’s view, insufficient standing alone.  
             The off-site PCE plume is the defining issue and was missed.         It triggers vapor intrusion, the contiguous property owner defense, GMZ land-use restrictions, and Lender decline risk — yet it was not classified as a REC.  
             Document-production quality control failed.         Three of eight documents are for a different property (Issue 1). This must be corrected before any party relies on the record.  
      
        9. SECONDARY ANALYSIS: DAYTON-PROPERTY DOCUMENTS (DIFFERENT PROPERTY — DO NOT RELY FOR THE TOLEDO ACQUISITION)  
      As explained in Issue 1, three documents in the production pertain to             2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414         (Montgomery County), owned by             Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.         — a different property, different seller, different price ($4,200,000), and different consultant (Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc.). These documents are reviewed here only because they were produced and contain their own issues;             they must not be applied to the Toledo acquisition.         If Cornerstone is separately pursuing the Dayton property, the following issues apply to that transaction.  
       Dayton Issue D1 — Tank 3 (4,000-gallon waste oil UST) closure never completed [CRITICAL for the Dayton transaction]  
          Description and evidence.         The Redland UST Closure Report (November 12, 2004) documents removal of three USTs at the Dayton property in September 2004. Tank 1 (10,000-gal diesel) and Tank 2 (10,000-gal diesel) were closed with confirmation sampling below BUSTR action levels (Tank 2 required over-excavation of 85 cubic yards; initial DRO up to 1,250 mg/kg, post-over-excavation max 280 mg/kg).             Tank 3 (4,000-gallon waste oil UST), however, was never closed.         Field observations during Tank 3 removal documented: moderate-to-heavy external corrosion;             one visible through-wall perforation (approximately 1.5 inches) on the tank bottom    ; approximately 4 inches of dark, viscous, oily residue; and             stained soil (dark black, strong petroleum/waste oil odor) at the excavation base and along the lower ~2 feet of the south and west sidewalls    . The seven confirmation soil samples for Tank 3 were collected but             analytical results were pending at the time of the report and were never received.         The report states:         “    A supplemental report will be submitted for Tank 3 upon receipt.    ”         An editor’s note confirms:         “        No supplemental report addressing Tank 3 analytical results was located in the seller’s project files, in the files of Redland Environmental Services, or in publicly available Ohio EPA records.    ”         The Tank 3 excavation was only             temporarily backfilled         (without compaction control) and paved over. The Terravista ESA confirms that closure letters were provided for Tank 1 and Tank 2 but             not for Tank 3         (the facility manager was         “    confident    ”         it was issued but could not locate it).  
          Consequence.         The waste oil UST had a through-wall perforation and visibly stained, odorous soil, but no confirmation analytical data, no supplemental report, and no closure letter exist. The closure was never completed. The 2011 VAP NFA letter for         “    Parcel A    ”         describes the area as the         “    former diesel underground storage tank area and associated petroleum-impacted soils    ”         — which may             not         encompass the waste-oil Tank 3 area. Waste oil (used crankcase oil, cutting oils, hydraulic fluids) may contain metals and other contaminants beyond petroleum. This is an unaddressed release for the Dayton property.  
          Recommendation (Dayton only).         Require a Phase II investigation of the former Tank 3 area (soil and groundwater sampling for petroleum hydrocarbons, metals, and waste-oil constituents); obtain or reconstruct the Tank 3 closure documentation; confirm whether the 2011 VAP NFA letter covers the Tank 3 area.  
        Dayton Issue D2 — REC-1 (western yard former ASTs) and REC-2 (Building 2 electroplating floor staining) [HIGH for Dayton]  
      The Terravista ESA identified two RECs for the Dayton property: (i) stressed/absent vegetation and darkened soil (~40 ft × 60 ft) in the western yard at the location of former Dayton Forge & Die above-ground storage tanks (1942 Sanborn         “    oil storage    ”         annotation); and (ii) floor staining (~15 ft × 20 ft) in Building 2 near the chemical drum storage area, where active chromium/nickel electroplating has occurred since 1987. Phase II investigation is recommended for both. (These findings apply to the Dayton property only.)  
        Dayton Issue D3 — HREC-2 (Central Valley Chemical Co.) likely misclassified [HIGH for Dayton]  
          Description and evidence.         The Terravista ESA classifies the former Central Valley Chemical Co. operations (Building 3, ~1975–1981, chemical blending of industrial cleaning solvents and degreasers including chlorinated solvents) as an HREC, relying on the 2011 VAP NFA letter. However, the ESA elsewhere states the NFA letter         “    covers a 2.3-acre area designated as Parcel A in the southern portion of the site    ”         and         “    does not address environmental conditions in areas of the Subject Property outside the Parcel A boundary.    ”         Building 3 (where Central Valley Chemical operated) is in the northeastern portion of the site,             outside         Parcel A. The ESA is internally inconsistent: it bases the HREC classification on a NFA letter that, by its own description, does not cover the Building 3 area. Additional evidence of a potential release at Building 3 includes: the 1971 aerial photograph showing ~100+ drums in outdoor storage on the north side of Building 3 (no secondary containment); a 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical (details not available — Ohio EPA file review pending); exterior staining (~8 ft × 12 ft) on the Building 3 loading dock; and floor cracking, patching, and ~3 inches of differential settlement inside Building 3.  
          Consequence.         Classifying Central Valley Chemical as an HREC implies the condition is resolved. But the NFA letter does not cover Building 3, and there is evidence of a potential chlorinated-solvent release (drum storage, enforcement action, staining, floor deterioration). This is likely a misclassification; the condition may warrant REC status and Phase II investigation (including vapor intrusion assessment for chlorinated solvents).  
          Recommendation (Dayton only).         Reclassify Central Valley Chemical as a REC (or at minimum a significant data gap); conduct a Phase II at Building 3 (soil, groundwater, soil gas for chlorinated solvents); obtain the 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement records (see D4).  
        Dayton Issue D4 — Ohio EPA file review not received; 1981 enforcement action unreviewed [MODERATE for Dayton]  
      The Terravista ESA requested Ohio EPA files on March 10, 2025, but had not received them as of the April 18, 2025 report date. The 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical Co. was therefore not reviewed. This is a data gap for the Dayton property.  
        Dayton Issue D5 — Partially buried 55-gallon drum (northwest boundary) not classified [MODERATE for Dayton]  
      A partially buried, significantly corroded 55-gallon drum with no visible label was observed near the northwest property boundary. The facility manager was unaware of its origin. The Terravista ESA noted it but did not classify it as a REC. A buried drum of unknown contents on the property warrants investigation and proper removal.  
        Dayton Issue D6 — Unlabeled older electrical transformer (Building 3) — potential PCB concern [MODERATE for Dayton]  
      One pad-mounted transformer adjacent to Building 3 had no PCB label (the other three transformers on site were labeled         “    Non-PCB    ”    ). The unlabeled unit appeared older with surface corrosion. No leaking was observed. The absence of a PCB label on an older transformer warrants verification of PCB status with the utility (Dayton Power & Grid Co.).  
        Dayton Issue D7 — ASTM E1527-13 (older standard) used instead of E1527-21 [MODERATE for Dayton]  
      The Terravista ESA was prepared to ASTM E1527-13, not the current E1527-21. While E1527-13 may still satisfy the AAI rule, the current standard is E1527-21. If the Dayton property is being acquired with lender financing or BFPP-defense reliance, confirm that E1527-13 is acceptable to the lender and sufficient for AAI; an update to E1527-21 may be warranted.  
        Dayton Issue D8 — GeoSearch RCRA waste codes and report-number discrepancy [LOW for Dayton]  
      The GeoSearch database summary lists RCRA waste codes for Valtec as D006 (cadmium), D007 (chromium), F001 (spent halogenated solvents used in degreasing), and F002 (spent halogenated solvents). The Terravista ESA, however, describes Valtec’s hazardous waste as         “    spent plating solutions, wastewater treatment sludge (F006), and miscellaneous solvent-contaminated rags and wipes    ”         — and does not mention F001/F002 halogenated solvents. The F001/F002 codes suggest historical chlorinated-solvent use/degreasing at the Dayton property that the ESA does not fully discuss. Additionally, the Terravista ESA references GeoSearch Report No. GS-2025-11847, while the GeoSearch summary lists Project/Order No. GS-2025-04817 and Inquiry No. INQ-OH-0328-7792 — a report-number discrepancy that should be reconciled.  
        Dayton Issue D9 — Non-scope items (ACM, LBP) [LOW for Dayton]  
      The Terravista ESA noted potential ACM (Buildings 1 and 2, constructed 1942 and 1955) and potential LBP (Buildings 1, 2, 3, and 5, all pre-1978) as non-scope items. Separate surveys are recommended before renovation/demolition.  
      
         10. PRIORITIZED RECOMMENDATIONS  
       Immediate (before the October 31, 2024 diligence deadline)  
              Segregate the Dayton documents         from the Toledo file and confirm with the deal team whether they were produced in error or pertain to a separate transaction (Issue 1).  
              Demand a supplemented/amended ESA         that (a) classifies the off-site PCE plume as a REC (Issue 2), (b) classifies the two unregistered USTs as RECs (Issue 8), (c) classifies the former drum storage area as a REC (Issue 9), and (d) reclassifies the 500-gal heating oil UST from HREC to REC (Issue 12). This is necessary to satisfy the Lender’s § 2 requirement.  
              Demand that Seller amend Schedules 4.12(a), 4.12(c), and 4.13         to disclose the 1988 spill, the 2003 NOV/consent order, the PCE plume/GMZ, and the two additional USTs (Issue 7).  
              Submit the Ohio EPA Central Office records request         for the 2003 consent order file (File No. NW-2003-0471) immediately, allowing 30–45 business days (Issue 11).  
              Negotiate a written extension of the Environmental Due Diligence Period         beyond October 31, 2024, to allow Phase II cost estimates to inform the § 6.3(b) termination decision (Issue 14). If no extension is granted, evaluate by October 31 whether a termination notice is warranted.  
              Begin PLL insurance underwriting in parallel         — it is non-negotiable and on the critical path (Issue 5).  
        Before closing (December 15, 2024)  
              Commission a Phase II ESA         scoped to all RECs (TCE degreasing area and drum; hexavalent chromium electroplating room and sump; off-site PCE plume with on-Property groundwater wells; the two unregistered USTs via geophysical survey; the former drum storage area; and a vapor intrusion assessment), with the three-scenario remediation cost estimates the Lender requires (Issues 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 15).  
              Characterize and remove the abandoned waste-TCE drum         and stained soil behind the maintenance building (Issue 3).  
              Secure/repair monitoring well MW-1         and investigate its origin and any associated data (Issue 10).  
              Renegotiate the indemnity package    : increase the cap and/or create an environmental-specific carve-out; extend the survival period; require Seller to fund a portion of the PLL premium and/or escrow (Issue 6).  
              Establish the remediation escrow         (if most-likely costs exceed $250,000) and the remediation plan (if costs exceed $500,000), per Lender §§ 3.B.3–3.B.4 (Issue 5).  
              Confirm Lender decline-trigger exposure    : assess worst-case remediation cost vs. 50% of appraised value (§ 3.C(a)); confirm no active RCRA corrective action (§ 3.C(c)); and close all data gaps practicable (§ 3.C(d)) (Issues 5, 11, 13).  
              Commission ACM and LBP surveys         before any renovation/demolition (Issue 20).  
        Post-closing / continuing obligations  
              Implement a continuing-obligations plan         to preserve CERCLA defenses: take reasonable steps regarding identified contamination; comply with GMZ restrictions; cooperate with the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning VAP parties and Ohio EPA; maintain the PLL insurance for the full 10-year term; comply with the Lender’s post-closing covenants (release notification, no UST activity without consent, annual certifications) (Issues 2, 5, 7).  
              Preserve all fraud/intentional misrepresentation arguments         given the apparent inaccuracy of the Seller’s environmental representations — the cap, basket, and survival period do not apply to fraud (Issues 6, 7).  
      
         11. LIMITATIONS  
      This memorandum is based solely on the eight documents listed in Section 2.1, which were provided for review. We did not independently verify the accuracy of the database reports, regulatory correspondence, or the Phase I ESA’s field observations, and we did not conduct an independent site reconnaissance or sampling. The severity ratings reflect our professional judgment based on the record as produced and are intended to assist the Buyer and its counsel in prioritizing due-diligence and negotiation actions; they are not a guarantee of any particular environmental outcome, remediation cost, or regulatory determination. The identification of conditions that         “    should have been classified as RECs    ”         reflects our review against ASTM E1527-21 and the Lender’s requirements; the environmental consultant of record is responsible for the ESA’s classifications, and any reclassification should be formally issued by a qualified Environmental Professional. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared in anticipation of litigation and in connection with the proposed transaction, and should not be disclosed to third parties without the consent of the Buyer and its counsel.  
      
          End of Memorandum.

content.text: Employment failures are about company-internal policy facts (Greenleaf's policy: $25K liquidated damages, 12-month non-compete cap, mutual confidentiality, dates/title-only references, no re-employment eligibility, regulatory carve-out for truthful info) plus ORS 656.236, 409A, tax indemnification, IP assignment, board-approved settlement range. 1	### FILE: cover-memo-to-gc.docx
2	         PRIVILEGED         AND         CONFIDENTIAL         —         ATTORNEY-CLIENT         COMMUNICATION         /         ATTORNEY         WORK         PRODUCT  
3	       MEMORANDUM  
4	                          TO:  
5	          Tyler Huang, General Counsel, Greenleaf Organic Foods, Inc.  
6	                FROM:  
7	          Outside Employment Counsel  
8	                DATE:  
9	          May 12, 2025  
10	                RE:  
11	          Review of Draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims (Delano v. Greenleaf) — Issues, Risks, and Redlined Revisions  
12	                PRIVILEGE:  
13	          Privileged & Confidential — Attorney-Client Communication / Attorney Work Product. Prepared at the request of, and for the benefit of, Greenleaf Organic Foods, Inc.  
14	         
15	       I. Executive Summary  
16	      You asked us to review the draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims dated May 5, 2025 (the         “    Draft    ”    ), prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C. on behalf of Marcus R. Delano, against Greenleaf’s employment records and the company policies and plan documents referenced in the Draft. A redlined markup reflecting our recommended revisions accompanies this memorandum as              settlement-agreement-markup.docx    .  
17	      The Draft is a $525,000 settlement of Mr. Delano’s retaliation, whistleblower, and age-discrimination claims arising from his November 8, 2024 internal cold-chain complaint, his December 3, 2024 Oregon OSHA complaint (No. OR-OSHA-2024-11872), and his March 14, 2025 termination. We identified             twenty-five (25) distinct issues    , ranging from defects that would render the age-discrimination release invalid to a material misstatement in the equity valuation to a significant piece of pretext evidence in Greenleaf’s own records.  
18	      The most important conclusions are:  
19	             The ADEA/age-discrimination release is likely invalid as drafted.         Mr. Delano is 58 years old (DOB 07/14/1966) and is protected by the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (    “    OWBPA    ”    ). The Draft gives him only             14 days         to consider the agreement (Sections 5(c), 16) and is             entirely silent on the required 7-day revocation period    . OWBPA requires at least             21 days         to consider and a             7-day revocation period    . As written, Mr. Delano could sign the agreement and still sue Greenleaf for age discrimination. This is the single most important defect and has been redlined.  
20	             The RSU valuation in the Draft is wrong and contains a false statement.         Section 3(d) values 5,000 accelerated RSUs at             $10.00/share ($50,000)    , stating this is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation conducted by Ridgepoint Valuation Services.    ”         Greenleaf’s equity records show the most recent 409A valuation is dated             January 15, 2025 at $12.50/share    . The correct value is             $62,500    , and the correct total settlement value is             $537,500, not $525,000         — a             $12,500 understatement    . The Draft’s statement that $10.00 is the         “    most recent    ”         valuation is false. This has been redlined.  
21	             Greenleaf’s own records contain powerful pretext evidence.         The employment summary shows that             Raj Mehta was hired as Senior Director of Supply Chain & Logistics on March 20, 2025 — six days after Mr. Delano’s March 14, 2025 termination — at $185,000.         The termination was cited as         “    elimination of position due to restructuring.    ”         Hiring a replacement within days directly contradicts the stated rationale and substantially strengthens Mr. Delano’s retaliation and age-discrimination claims. This strongly supports the decision to settle, but it also makes the non-disparagement and reference provisions (which encode the         “    restructuring    ”         narrative) fragile.  
22	             The Draft is missing several provisions essential to protect Greenleaf    , including any governing-law/venue/dispute-resolution clause, a return-of-property clause, a confidential-information/trade-secret clause, a cooperation clause, and any address of Greenleaf’s EPLI insurance. These have been added in the redline.  
23	             Several provisions are unenforceable or unlawful as drafted    , including the non-compete’s coverage of             California         (void under Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600) and the confidentiality/non-disparagement clauses’ failure to carve out the right to file with or cooperate with government agencies (EEOC, OSHA, SEC, NLRB, Oregon BOLI).  
24	      We recommend that Greenleaf             not execute the Draft in its current form    . The accompanying redline addresses the defects we identified; the items below that require factual follow-up (the 2024 bonus, EPLI consent-to-settle, Compensation Committee approval) should be resolved before execution.  
25	      
26	        II. Documents Reviewed  
27	              Draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims    , dated May 5, 2025, prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C. (the         “    Draft    ”    ).  
28	              Greenleaf Employment Summary for Marcus R. Delano         (Employee ID GRN-2017-0438), prepared by Leanne Foss, HR Director, dated May 10, 2025, containing: (a) Employment History; (b) Compensation History (2017–2024); and (c) Equity Details, including the RSU Grant Summary, Vesting Schedule, 409A Valuation History, Status at Termination, and Settlement Proposal Analysis (the         “    Employment Records    ”    ).  
29	              Company policies and plan documents referenced in the Draft    : the Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan and the RSU Grant Agreement dated July 1, 2022 (as documented in the Equity Details tab of the Employment Records).  
30	      
31	        III. Summary of Redline Revisions  
32	      The redline (       settlement-agreement-markup.docx    ) contains tracked insertions and deletions. The principal revisions are:  
33	                          #  
34	          Location  
35	          Revision  
36	            1  
37	          §1(b), Preamble  
38	          Effective Date redefined as the 8th day after the last signature, following the 7-day revocation period (no longer fixed at May 5, 2025).  
39	            2  
40	          §2  
41	          Qualified the         “    all wages/compensation received    ”         representation to acknowledge the back-pay payment includes any unpaid 2024 incentive comp and PTO; removed         “    not in dispute    ”         language.  
42	            3  
43	          §3(b)  
44	          Clarified back pay includes unpaid 2024 performance incentive compensation.  
45	            4  
46	          §3(d)  
47	          Corrected RSU value to $12.50/share (Jan. 15, 2025 409A) = $62,500; added Compensation Committee approval as a condition precedent with a $62,500 cash fallback.  
48	            5  
49	          §3(e)  
50	          Corrected total settlement value to $537,500.  
51	            6  
52	          §4 (new ¶)  
53	          Added government-agency / whistleblower carve-out (right to file, cooperate, and receive awards; OSHA investigation unaffected).  
54	            7  
55	          §5(c)  
56	          Changed consideration period from 14 to 21 days.  
57	            8  
58	          §5(g) (new)  
59	          Added 7-day revocation period (OWBPA).  
60	            9  
61	          §6(iv)–(v)  
62	          Added government-agency reporting carve-out to confidentiality.  
63	            10  
64	          §7  
65	          Added government-agency/truthful-statement carve-out; changed perpetual duration to 3 years.  
66	            11  
67	          §9  
68	          Removed California from non-compete; added ORS 653.295 acknowledgment and California carve-out.  
69	            12  
70	          §11  
71	          Softened         “    positive    ”         reference to         “    neutral, factual    ”    ; revised mandated praise.  
72	            13  
73	          §15(a)(ii)  
74	          Added government-agency carve-out to the no-future-charges representation.  
75	            14  
76	          §16  
77	          Changed consideration period to 21 days; added revocation-period mechanics.  
78	            15  
79	          §18  
80	          Carved out the Equity Plan, RSU Grant, and prior confidentiality/restrictive-covenant agreements from the supersession clause.  
81	            16  
82	          §19 (new)  
83	          Return of Company Property (with 401(k) information).  
84	            17  
85	          §20 (new)  
86	          Confidential Information and Trade Secrets (with DTSA immunity notice).  
87	            18  
88	          §21 (new)  
89	          Cooperation.  
90	            19  
91	          §22 (new)  
92	          Insurance (EPLI cooperation; payments unconditional).  
93	            20  
94	          §23 (new)  
95	          Governing Law, Venue, and Jury Waiver.  
96	         
97	        IV. Issues and Risks  
98	      Each issue below is rated             Critical    ,             High    ,             Medium    , or             Lower         based on its potential effect on the enforceability of the release, Greenleaf’s financial exposure, or Greenleaf’s litigation risk.  
99	       Issue 1 — ADEA/OWBPA release is likely invalid: 14-day consideration period and no revocation period. [Critical]  
100	          The problem.         Mr. Delano is 58 years old (DOB 07/14/1966; age 58 as of the report date of May 10, 2025). His age-discrimination claim under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (    “    ADEA    ”    ) is the core of the settlement. For a release of ADEA claims to be         “    knowing and voluntary    ”         under the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (    “    OWBPA    ”    ), 29 U.S.C. § 626(f), the agreement must (a) give the individual             at least 21 days         to consider the agreement (45 days only if the program is part of a group exit-incentive or other termination program — this is an individual settlement, so 21 days applies), and (b) allow             at least 7 days to revoke         after signing, during which the agreement is not effective or enforceable.  
101	      The Draft provides only             fourteen (14) calendar days         to consider (Sections 5(c) and 16) and is             entirely silent on any revocation period    . Neither requirement is satisfied.  
102	          Consequence.         The ADEA release is almost certainly invalid. Mr. Delano could execute the agreement, accept the $525,000, and thereafter file an ADEA charge and lawsuit, arguing the waiver was not knowing and voluntary. Greenleaf would have paid the consideration and received no enforceable release of the age claim — the most valuable claim in the settlement. This is the single greatest risk in the Draft.  
103	          Redline / recommendation.         Section 5(c) and Section 16 revised to             21 days    ; new Section 5(g) adds the             7-day revocation period         with revocation mechanics; the Effective Date (Section 1(b)) is redefined as the eighth day after the last signature (i.e., after the revocation period expires without revocation). These changes protect Greenleaf by making the ADEA release enforceable.  
104	        Issue 2 — RSU valuation uses an outdated 409A valuation and contains a false statement; total settlement value understated by $12,500. [Critical]  
105	          The problem.         Section 3(d) values the 5,000 accelerated RSUs at             Ten Dollars ($10.00) per share    , stating this is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation conducted by Ridgepoint Valuation Services,    ”         yielding a total RSU value of             $50,000    . Section 3(e) aggregates the total settlement value at             $525,000    .  
106	      Greenleaf’s Equity Details tell a different story. The 409A Valuation History (Sub-Table C) shows four valuations by Ridgepoint:  
107	                          Valuation Date  
108	          FMV/Share  
109	          Notes  
110	            July 1, 2022  
111	          $8.00  
112	          At time of RSU grant  
113	            July 1, 2023  
114	          $9.25  
115	          Annual valuation  
116	            July 1, 2024  
117	          $10.00  
118	          Annual valuation  
119	                January 15, 2025  
120	              $12.50  
121	              Most recent 409A valuation — CURRENT  
122	         The             January 15, 2025         valuation at             $12.50/share         is the most recent. The Draft’s statement that $10.00 is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation    ”         is             false    . The Settlement Proposal Analysis (Sub-Table E) in Greenleaf’s own records expressly flags this:         “    Draft settlement agreement uses July 2024 409A valuation ($10.00/share). The most recent 409A valuation performed by Ridgepoint Valuation Services dated January 15, 2025 values shares at $12.50/share. The correct value of 5,000 accelerated RSUs is $62,500, not $50,000. The corrected total settlement value should be $537,500 instead of $525,000.    ”  
123	          Consequence.         (i) The agreement contains a material misstatement of fact that could support a fraudulent-inducement or mutual-mistake challenge to the RSU provision (and, by extension, the release). (ii) The total consideration is understated by             $12,500         ($62,500 − $50,000), and the total settlement value should be             $537,500    , not $525,000. (iii) Using a stale 409A value for a settlement may also create tax-reporting and 409A-compliance concerns.  
124	          Redline / recommendation.         Section 3(d) corrected to             $12.50/share    , expressly referencing the             January 15, 2025         409A valuation, with a total RSU value of             $62,500    ; Section 3(e) corrected to             $537,500    . Confirm with the plan administrator and tax counsel that the corrected value and reporting are consistent with the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan and 409A.  
125	        Issue 3 — RSU acceleration is unconditional; the plan requires Compensation Committee approval. [High]  
126	          The problem.         Section 3(d) states that         “    Greenleaf shall cause the immediate accelerated vesting    ”         of 5,000 RSUs, with no condition. The Equity Details (Sub-Table A) state that the         “    Compensation Committee has discretion to approve accelerated vesting in connection with separation agreements    ”         under the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, and the Forfeiture Provision provides that unvested RSUs are forfeited upon termination unless an exception (death, disability, Change of Control) applies — none of which is present here. Acceleration is therefore not automatic; it requires Compensation Committee action.  
127	          Consequence.         If the Compensation Committee declines to approve the acceleration, Greenleaf cannot deliver the shares and would be in breach of the agreement as drafted, exposing Greenleaf to a breach-of-contract claim (and potentially reviving the released claims if the breach is material). The Draft gives Greenleaf no protection.  
128	          Redline / recommendation.         Section 3(d) revised to make accelerated vesting             subject to Compensation Committee approval         as an express condition precedent, with Greenleaf’s obligation to seek approval promptly, and a             cash fallback of $62,500         (at Mr. Delano’s election) if approval is not obtained within 30 days.             Action item:         obtain Compensation Committee approval (or document the process) before or promptly after execution.  
129	        Issue 4 — Greenleaf’s records contain strong pretext evidence: a replacement was hired six days after termination. [Critical — litigation risk]  
130	          The problem.         The Employment History states that Mr. Delano was terminated on             March 14, 2025    , with the stated reason         “    Elimination of position due to restructuring of supply chain division.    ”         The same record states:         “        Replacement Hire: Raj Mehta, Senior Director of Supply Chain & Logistics, hired March 20, 2025, salary $185,000.    ”         A replacement was therefore hired             six days         after the termination, in the same functional area (supply chain and logistics), at a salary ($185,000) below Mr. Delano’s $218,000 base.  
131	          Consequence.         This is powerful evidence that the         “    elimination of position    ”         rationale is pretextual. It directly supports Mr. Delano’s retaliation claim under ORS 654.062(5) (he complained internally on November 8, 2024 and to Oregon OSHA on December 3, 2024, and was placed on leave February 10, 2025 and terminated March 14, 2025) and his ADEA claim (he was replaced by a lower-paid individual; the timing is suspect). The close temporal proximity between protected activity and adverse action, combined with a near-immediate replacement, makes this a strong plaintiff case. Two practical implications:  
132	              The settlement is well-advised.         Greenleaf’s litigation exposure (back pay, front pay, compensatory and (under ORS 659A) punitive damages, attorney fees, and Oregon OSHA penalties) likely exceeds the $537,500 corrected value. Settling is the right call.  
133	              The                 “        restructuring        ”                 narrative is fragile.         Sections 7 (non-disparagement) and 11 (reference) encode the         “    restructuring/elimination of position    ”         story. If the Raj Mehta hire surfaces — through the pending Oregon OSHA investigation, public records, LinkedIn, or a prospective-employer reference check — the narrative collapses and Greenleaf’s good-faith position is undermined. Greenleaf should ensure the release is airtight and should not rely on the non-disparagement/reference provisions to suppress facts that are already in the company’s own records.  
134	          Redline / recommendation.         No redline can redact this evidence. We recommend: (i) confirm the release covers all claims arising from the replacement hire and the termination rationale; (ii) preserve litigation hold / document-retention for the OSHA investigation; (iii) consider whether the reference provision (Section 11) should avoid affirmatively stating a         “    reorganization    ”         reason that the records contradict (the redline softens the reference to         “    neutral, factual    ”         and states the position         “    was eliminated as part of a reorganization    ”         — Greenleaf should satisfy itself that this statement is defensible, or further limit the reference to dates and title only).  
135	        Issue 5 — No governing law, venue, or dispute-resolution provision. [High]  
136	          The problem.         The Draft contains no choice-of-law clause, no forum-selection/venue clause, no jury waiver, and no arbitration clause. Disputes about enforcement (e.g., alleged breach of the non-compete, confidentiality, or non-disparagement provisions) could therefore be brought in an uncertain forum under uncertain law.  
137	          Consequence.         Greenleaf loses predictability and home-court advantage, and may face a jury in an employment-friendly forum on a breach claim. This is a significant gap for a company that is imposing post-termination restrictive covenants.  
138	          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 23 added:             Oregon law         governs; exclusive venue in             Multnomah County, Oregon         state court or the             U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon    ; mutual             jury-trial waiver    ; and fee-shifting to the prevailing party. (If Greenleaf prefers arbitration, we can substitute an arbitration clause; confirm preference.)  
139	        Issue 6 — Non-compete covers California, where non-competes are void. [High]  
140	          The problem.         Section 9 restricts Mr. Delano from working for an organic-food competitor         “    within the states of Oregon, Washington, Idaho,             and California    .    ”         California Business and Professions Code § 16600 voids contractual restraints on the practice of a lawful profession, trade, or business, subject only to narrow exceptions (sale of a business; certain partner/LLC member departures) that do not apply here. A non-compete covering California is unenforceable as to California.  
141	          Consequence.         The California portion is void; including it invites a challenge to the entire non-compete and creates a severability dispute. It also signals overreach that a court may hold against Greenleaf when evaluating the reasonableness of the remaining geographic scope.  
142	          Redline / recommendation.         Section 9 revised to remove California from the restricted territory and to add an express carve-out stating the non-compete does not apply in California to the extent prohibited by Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600. The severability clause (Section 17) is retained.  
143	        Issue 7 — Confidentiality and non-disparagement lack government-agency / whistleblower carve-outs. [High]  
144	          The problem.         Section 6 (confidentiality) permits disclosure only to spouse, counsel, tax advisor, or         “    as required by applicable law, regulation, court order, subpoena, or other compulsory legal process.    ”         Section 7 (non-disparagement) is broadly worded. Section 15(a)(ii) states Mr. Delano         “    agrees not to file any… charge, or proceeding in the future with respect to any claim released herein, except as may be required by law or to enforce the terms of this Agreement.    ”         Read together, these provisions could be construed to prohibit Mr. Delano from             voluntarily         filing a charge with, or cooperating in an investigation by, the EEOC, Oregon OSHA, the NLRB, the SEC, or the Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries, and from receiving a whistleblower award.  
145	      Such restrictions are unenforceable and, in some respects, unlawful: federal anti-retalization and whistleblower statutes protect the right to report to government agencies and to receive awards (e.g., SEC/Dodd-Frank; OSHA); the EEOC’s position is that agreements cannot bar charges or EEOC cooperation; and the NLRB has scrutinized overbroad confidentiality/non-disparagement clauses. The point is acute here because the             Oregon OSHA investigation (Complaint No. OR-OSHA-2024-11872) is pending and unresolved    , and Mr. Delano cannot waive the government’s right to investigate.  
146	          Consequence.         Without carve-outs, these provisions are vulnerable to challenge, and Greenleaf risks an enforcement action or a public-policy defense that could invalidate portions of the agreement. The provisions also create a trap for Mr. Delano that a court is unlikely to enforce.  
147	          Redline / recommendation.         Carve-outs added: a new paragraph at the end of Section 4 (right to file, cooperate, and receive awards; OSHA investigation unaffected); a new exception (v) in Section 6; a carve-out in Section 7; and a cross-reference in Section 15(a)(ii). The carve-outs preserve the confidentiality of the settlement terms while protecting lawful government reporting.  
148	        Issue 8 — 2024 performance bonus was never determined or paid; potential unpaid-wage claim. [High]  
149	          The problem.         The Compensation History shows a 2024 bonus target of             20% of $218,000 = $43,600    , with the actual bonus recorded as         “        Not determined — termination    ”         and         “    2024 bonus not determined prior to termination.    ”         Historically, bonuses were paid in March of the following year (e.g., 2023 bonus paid March 2024). Mr. Delano was placed on administrative leave February 10, 2025 and terminated March 14, 2025 — before any 2024 bonus was determined or paid.  
150	      Section 2 of the Draft states that Mr. Delano         “    confirms that he has received all wages, salary, and compensation earned through the Separation Date, including payment for all accrued but unused paid time off (    ‘    PTO    ’    ), and that no further wages, salary, commissions, bonuses, or benefits of any kind are owed,    ”         and that         “    the terms of this Section 2 are not in dispute.    ”         If the 2024 bonus was an             earned wage         under Greenleaf’s bonus plan, this representation is false, and the release of wage claims may not fully resolve the exposure.  
151	          Consequence.         Under Oregon wage statutes (ORS 653.045; ORS 652.610), unpaid wages can carry             penalty wages of up to 30 days’ wages         plus the employee’s attorney fees and costs. A $43,600 unpaid bonus could therefore generate a substantially larger liability if litigated. The blanket         “    all compensation received    ”         representation also creates a mutual-mistake/fraud risk.  
152	          Redline / recommendation.         Section 2 revised to acknowledge that the Section 3 payments include, by mutual agreement, any unpaid 2024 incentive compensation and PTO, and to remove the         “    not in dispute    ”         language; Section 3(b) clarified that back pay includes unpaid 2024 incentive compensation.             Action item:         HR should confirm, before execution, whether the 2024 bonus was earned/vested under the bonus plan (e.g., whether the plan requires employment at the payment date) and document the basis for treating it as satisfied by the back-pay component.

154	          The problem.         The Preamble states the agreement is         “    entered into as of May 5, 2025 (    ‘    Effective Date    ’    ),    ”         and Section 1(b) defines         “    Effective Date    ”         as         “    May 5, 2025, the date first set forth above.    ”         But Mr. Delano has up to 21 days to sign (after the redline) and 7 days to revoke, so the agreement cannot be effective on May 5, 2025. Section 1(b) redefined so the Effective Date is the             eighth (8th) calendar day after the last Party signs    , provided Mr. Delano has not revoked during the 7-day Revocation Period; the Preamble updated to reference the defined Effective Date rather than a fixed date. This aligns payment timing with OWBPA mechanics.

241	      This memorandum is based on the Draft, the Employment Records, and the plan/policy information reflected in those records. We have not independently reviewed the full Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, the RSU Grant Agreement, the EPLI policy, the bonus plan, any prior employment or confidentiality agreements, or Greenleaf’s personnel policies beyond what is reflected in the Employment Records. Our review is limited to the issues identified from these materials and does not constitute a comprehensive audit of all of Mr. Delano’s claims or of Greenleaf’s employment practices. Statutory citations and requirements (including OWBPA, ORS 653.295, ORS 652.610/653.045, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600, and 18 U.S.C. § 1833(b)) are summarized from the referenced sources and should be confirmed against current text before reliance. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared for Greenleaf in anticipation of settlement, and should not be disclosed to Mr. Delano or his counsel except as Greenleaf determines appropriate in the negotiation.

366	            This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous negotiations, representations, agreements, promises, and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter, including but not limited to any prior offer letters, employment agreements, memoranda of understanding, or term sheets exchanged between the Parties in connection with the resolution of the disputes addressed herein          ; provided, however, that this Agreement shall not supersede or modify the terms of the Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, the RSU Grant Agreement dated July 1, 2022, or any prior confidentiality, invention assignment, or restrictive covenant agreement between the Parties, each of which shall remain in full force and effect in accordance with its terms except as expressly modified by this Agreement          . No amendment, modification, supplement, or waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by both Parties. No waiver of any breach or default under this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of any subsequent breach or default.  
367	                  Section 19. Return of Company Property   
368	                Delano represents and warrants that, prior to or concurrently with the Effective Date, he has returned to Greenleaf all property, documents, records, files, data, keys, access cards, identification badges, laptops, mobile devices, equipment, and other materials (in whatever form, whether electronic or physical) belonging to Greenleaf or containing Greenleaf confidential or proprietary information that were in his possession, custody, or control, and that he has not retained any copies, extracts, or summaries thereof. Delano shall permanently delete or destroy any Greenleaf information remaining on any personal devices, accounts, or storage media and shall provide Greenleaf with a written certification of such deletion and destruction within ten (10) calendar days of the Effective Date. Greenleaf shall provide Delano with information regarding his 401(k) account balance, vested employer matching contributions, and rollover or distribution options through Cascadia Benefit Administrators.   
369	                  Section 20. Confidential Information and Trade Secrets   
370	                Delano acknowledges that during his employment with Greenleaf he had access to and became familiar with confidential and proprietary information of Greenleaf, including without limitation information regarding supply chain operations, cold-chain protocols, supplier and vendor relationships, pricing, customer lists, business strategies, and financial information (collectively, "Confidential Information"). Delano agrees that he shall hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and shall not, at any time after the Separation Date, use, disclose, or make available to any person or entity any Confidential Information, except as required by applicable law. The obligations in this Section 20 shall survive the termination of this Agreement. Pursuant to the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (18 U.S.C. § 1833(b)), Delano is hereby notified that an individual shall not be held criminally or civilly liable under any federal or state trade secret law for the disclosure of a trade secret that is made (i) in confidence to a federal, state, or local government official or to an attorney, solely for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law, or (ii) in a complaint or other document filed in a lawsuit or other proceeding, if such filing is made under seal. Nothing in this Agreement is intended to conflict with 18 U.S.C. § 1833(b) or to limit Delano's right to receive a whistleblower award.   
371	                  Section 21. Cooperation   
372	                Following the Effective Date, Delano shall reasonably cooperate with Greenleaf in connection with any internal or external investigation, audit, inquiry, administrative proceeding, or litigation relating to matters within the scope of Delano's former responsibilities, including the pending Oregon OSHA investigation, provided that Greenleaf shall reimburse Delano for reasonable, documented out-of-pocket expenses incurred in connection with such cooperation and, for cooperation requiring substantial time, shall pay Delano reasonable compensation at a rate mutually agreed upon in advance. Greenleaf shall provide reasonable notice and shall endeavor to schedule any cooperation so as not to unreasonably interfere with Delano's other employment. Nothing in this Section 21 shall require Delano to cooperate in any matter in which Delano is a party adverse to Greenleaf, or to provide information or testimony that would violate Delano's rights under this Agreement or applicable law, or to disclose information protected by the attorney-client privilege or any other applicable privilege.   
373	                  Section 22. Insurance   
374	                Greenleaf represents that it has maintained employment practices liability insurance with Timberline Insurance Group (Policy No. TIG-EPL-2024-00391) that may be applicable to the claims resolved by this Agreement. Greenleaf shall be responsible for determining, in consultation with its insurers, whether any portion of the Settlement Payment is covered under such insurance and for obtaining any consents required under its insurance policies to enter into this Agreement. The Parties acknowledge that Greenleaf's obligations to make the payments and provide the benefits set forth in this Agreement are absolute and unconditional and are not conditioned upon the availability, approval, or payment of any insurance proceeds. Greenleaf shall provide Delano with reasonable cooperation, including execution of any release, proof of claim, or other document reasonably required by Greenleaf's insurer to process or confirm coverage.   
375	                  Section 23. Governing Law, Venue, and Jury Waiver   
376	                This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Oregon, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. Any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, or to the interpretation or enforcement hereof, shall be brought exclusively in the state courts located in Multnomah County, Oregon, or, if federal jurisdiction exists, in the United States District Court for the District of Oregon, and each Party hereby consents to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of such courts and waives any objection based on forum non conveniens. EACH PARTY KNOWINGLY AND VOLUNTARILY WAIVES ANY RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION, SUIT, OR PROCEEDING ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT. The prevailing Party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorney fees and costs.   
377	             IN WITNESS WHEREOF         , the Parties have executed this Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims as of the date first set forth above.  
378	             GREENLEAF ORGANIC FOODS, INC.  
379	            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
380	            Name (printed):           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
381	            Title:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
382	            Date:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
383	             MARCUS R. DELANO  
384	            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
385	            Name (printed): Marcus R. Delano  
386	            Date:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
387	             Approved as to Form:  
388	             ENGEL & ASSOCIATES, P.C.  
389	            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _
