### FILE: cover-memo-to-gc.docx
         PRIVILEGED         AND         CONFIDENTIAL         —         ATTORNEY-CLIENT         COMMUNICATION         /         ATTORNEY         WORK         PRODUCT  
       MEMORANDUM  
                          TO:  
          Tyler Huang, General Counsel, Greenleaf Organic Foods, Inc.  
                FROM:  
          Outside Employment Counsel  
                DATE:  
          May 12, 2025  
                RE:  
          Review of Draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims (Delano v. Greenleaf) — Issues, Risks, and Redlined Revisions  
                PRIVILEGE:  
          Privileged & Confidential — Attorney-Client Communication / Attorney Work Product. Prepared at the request of, and for the benefit of, Greenleaf Organic Foods, Inc.  
         
       I. Executive Summary  
      You asked us to review the draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims dated May 5, 2025 (the         “    Draft    ”    ), prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C. on behalf of Marcus R. Delano, against Greenleaf’s employment records and the company policies and plan documents referenced in the Draft. A redlined markup reflecting our recommended revisions accompanies this memorandum as              settlement-agreement-markup.docx    .  
      The Draft is a $525,000 settlement of Mr. Delano’s retaliation, whistleblower, and age-discrimination claims arising from his November 8, 2024 internal cold-chain complaint, his December 3, 2024 Oregon OSHA complaint (No. OR-OSHA-2024-11872), and his March 14, 2025 termination. We identified             twenty-five (25) distinct issues    , ranging from defects that would render the age-discrimination release invalid to a material misstatement in the equity valuation to a significant piece of pretext evidence in Greenleaf’s own records.  
      The most important conclusions are:  
             The ADEA/age-discrimination release is likely invalid as drafted.         Mr. Delano is 58 years old (DOB 07/14/1966) and is protected by the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (    “    OWBPA    ”    ). The Draft gives him only             14 days         to consider the agreement (Sections 5(c), 16) and is             entirely silent on the required 7-day revocation period    . OWBPA requires at least             21 days         to consider and a             7-day revocation period    . As written, Mr. Delano could sign the agreement and still sue Greenleaf for age discrimination. This is the single most important defect and has been redlined.  
             The RSU valuation in the Draft is wrong and contains a false statement.         Section 3(d) values 5,000 accelerated RSUs at             $10.00/share ($50,000)    , stating this is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation conducted by Ridgepoint Valuation Services.    ”         Greenleaf’s equity records show the most recent 409A valuation is dated             January 15, 2025 at $12.50/share    . The correct value is             $62,500    , and the correct total settlement value is             $537,500, not $525,000         — a             $12,500 understatement    . The Draft’s statement that $10.00 is the         “    most recent    ”         valuation is false. This has been redlined.  
             Greenleaf’s own records contain powerful pretext evidence.         The employment summary shows that             Raj Mehta was hired as Senior Director of Supply Chain & Logistics on March 20, 2025 — six days after Mr. Delano’s March 14, 2025 termination — at $185,000.         The termination was cited as         “    elimination of position due to restructuring.    ”         Hiring a replacement within days directly contradicts the stated rationale and substantially strengthens Mr. Delano’s retaliation and age-discrimination claims. This strongly supports the decision to settle, but it also makes the non-disparagement and reference provisions (which encode the         “    restructuring    ”         narrative) fragile.  
             The Draft is missing several provisions essential to protect Greenleaf    , including any governing-law/venue/dispute-resolution clause, a return-of-property clause, a confidential-information/trade-secret clause, a cooperation clause, and any address of Greenleaf’s EPLI insurance. These have been added in the redline.  
             Several provisions are unenforceable or unlawful as drafted    , including the non-compete’s coverage of             California         (void under Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600) and the confidentiality/non-disparagement clauses’ failure to carve out the right to file with or cooperate with government agencies (EEOC, OSHA, SEC, NLRB, Oregon BOLI).  
      We recommend that Greenleaf             not execute the Draft in its current form    . The accompanying redline addresses the defects we identified; the items below that require factual follow-up (the 2024 bonus, EPLI consent-to-settle, Compensation Committee approval) should be resolved before execution.  
      
        II. Documents Reviewed  
              Draft Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims    , dated May 5, 2025, prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C. (the         “    Draft    ”    ).  
              Greenleaf Employment Summary for Marcus R. Delano         (Employee ID GRN-2017-0438), prepared by Leanne Foss, HR Director, dated May 10, 2025, containing: (a) Employment History; (b) Compensation History (2017–2024); and (c) Equity Details, including the RSU Grant Summary, Vesting Schedule, 409A Valuation History, Status at Termination, and Settlement Proposal Analysis (the         “    Employment Records    ”    ).  
              Company policies and plan documents referenced in the Draft    : the Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan and the RSU Grant Agreement dated July 1, 2022 (as documented in the Equity Details tab of the Employment Records).  
      
        III. Summary of Redline Revisions  
      The redline (       settlement-agreement-markup.docx    ) contains tracked insertions and deletions. The principal revisions are:  
                          #  
          Location  
          Revision  
            1  
          §1(b), Preamble  
          Effective Date redefined as the 8th day after the last signature, following the 7-day revocation period (no longer fixed at May 5, 2025).  
            2  
          §2  
          Qualified the         “    all wages/compensation received    ”         representation to acknowledge the back-pay payment includes any unpaid 2024 incentive comp and PTO; removed         “    not in dispute    ”         language.  
            3  
          §3(b)  
          Clarified back pay includes unpaid 2024 performance incentive compensation.  
            4  
          §3(d)  
          Corrected RSU value to $12.50/share (Jan. 15, 2025 409A) = $62,500; added Compensation Committee approval as a condition precedent with a $62,500 cash fallback.  
            5  
          §3(e)  
          Corrected total settlement value to $537,500.  
            6  
          §4 (new ¶)  
          Added government-agency / whistleblower carve-out (right to file, cooperate, and receive awards; OSHA investigation unaffected).  
            7  
          §5(c)  
          Changed consideration period from 14 to 21 days.  
            8  
          §5(g) (new)  
          Added 7-day revocation period (OWBPA).  
            9  
          §6(iv)–(v)  
          Added government-agency reporting carve-out to confidentiality.  
            10  
          §7  
          Added government-agency/truthful-statement carve-out; changed perpetual duration to 3 years.  
            11  
          §9  
          Removed California from non-compete; added ORS 653.295 acknowledgment and California carve-out.  
            12  
          §11  
          Softened         “    positive    ”         reference to         “    neutral, factual    ”    ; revised mandated praise.  
            13  
          §15(a)(ii)  
          Added government-agency carve-out to the no-future-charges representation.  
            14  
          §16  
          Changed consideration period to 21 days; added revocation-period mechanics.  
            15  
          §18  
          Carved out the Equity Plan, RSU Grant, and prior confidentiality/restrictive-covenant agreements from the supersession clause.  
            16  
          §19 (new)  
          Return of Company Property (with 401(k) information).  
            17  
          §20 (new)  
          Confidential Information and Trade Secrets (with DTSA immunity notice).  
            18  
          §21 (new)  
          Cooperation.  
            19  
          §22 (new)  
          Insurance (EPLI cooperation; payments unconditional).  
            20  
          §23 (new)  
          Governing Law, Venue, and Jury Waiver.  
         
        IV. Issues and Risks  
      Each issue below is rated             Critical    ,             High    ,             Medium    , or             Lower         based on its potential effect on the enforceability of the release, Greenleaf’s financial exposure, or Greenleaf’s litigation risk.  
       Issue 1 — ADEA/OWBPA release is likely invalid: 14-day consideration period and no revocation period. [Critical]  
          The problem.         Mr. Delano is 58 years old (DOB 07/14/1966; age 58 as of the report date of May 10, 2025). His age-discrimination claim under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (    “    ADEA    ”    ) is the core of the settlement. For a release of ADEA claims to be         “    knowing and voluntary    ”         under the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (    “    OWBPA    ”    ), 29 U.S.C. § 626(f), the agreement must (a) give the individual             at least 21 days         to consider the agreement (45 days only if the program is part of a group exit-incentive or other termination program — this is an individual settlement, so 21 days applies), and (b) allow             at least 7 days to revoke         after signing, during which the agreement is not effective or enforceable.  
      The Draft provides only             fourteen (14) calendar days         to consider (Sections 5(c) and 16) and is             entirely silent on any revocation period    . Neither requirement is satisfied.  
          Consequence.         The ADEA release is almost certainly invalid. Mr. Delano could execute the agreement, accept the $525,000, and thereafter file an ADEA charge and lawsuit, arguing the waiver was not knowing and voluntary. Greenleaf would have paid the consideration and received no enforceable release of the age claim — the most valuable claim in the settlement. This is the single greatest risk in the Draft.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 5(c) and Section 16 revised to             21 days    ; new Section 5(g) adds the             7-day revocation period         with revocation mechanics; the Effective Date (Section 1(b)) is redefined as the eighth day after the last signature (i.e., after the revocation period expires without revocation). These changes protect Greenleaf by making the ADEA release enforceable.  
        Issue 2 — RSU valuation uses an outdated 409A valuation and contains a false statement; total settlement value understated by $12,500. [Critical]  
          The problem.         Section 3(d) values the 5,000 accelerated RSUs at             Ten Dollars ($10.00) per share    , stating this is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation conducted by Ridgepoint Valuation Services,    ”         yielding a total RSU value of             $50,000    . Section 3(e) aggregates the total settlement value at             $525,000    .  
      Greenleaf’s Equity Details tell a different story. The 409A Valuation History (Sub-Table C) shows four valuations by Ridgepoint:  
                          Valuation Date  
          FMV/Share  
          Notes  
            July 1, 2022  
          $8.00  
          At time of RSU grant  
            July 1, 2023  
          $9.25  
          Annual valuation  
            July 1, 2024  
          $10.00  
          Annual valuation  
                January 15, 2025  
              $12.50  
              Most recent 409A valuation — CURRENT  
         The             January 15, 2025         valuation at             $12.50/share         is the most recent. The Draft’s statement that $10.00 is         “    based on the most recent independent valuation    ”         is             false    . The Settlement Proposal Analysis (Sub-Table E) in Greenleaf’s own records expressly flags this:         “    Draft settlement agreement uses July 2024 409A valuation ($10.00/share). The most recent 409A valuation performed by Ridgepoint Valuation Services dated January 15, 2025 values shares at $12.50/share. The correct value of 5,000 accelerated RSUs is $62,500, not $50,000. The corrected total settlement value should be $537,500 instead of $525,000.    ”  
          Consequence.         (i) The agreement contains a material misstatement of fact that could support a fraudulent-inducement or mutual-mistake challenge to the RSU provision (and, by extension, the release). (ii) The total consideration is understated by             $12,500         ($62,500 − $50,000), and the total settlement value should be             $537,500    , not $525,000. (iii) Using a stale 409A value for a settlement may also create tax-reporting and 409A-compliance concerns.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 3(d) corrected to             $12.50/share    , expressly referencing the             January 15, 2025         409A valuation, with a total RSU value of             $62,500    ; Section 3(e) corrected to             $537,500    . Confirm with the plan administrator and tax counsel that the corrected value and reporting are consistent with the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan and 409A.  
        Issue 3 — RSU acceleration is unconditional; the plan requires Compensation Committee approval. [High]  
          The problem.         Section 3(d) states that         “    Greenleaf shall cause the immediate accelerated vesting    ”         of 5,000 RSUs, with no condition. The Equity Details (Sub-Table A) state that the         “    Compensation Committee has discretion to approve accelerated vesting in connection with separation agreements    ”         under the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, and the Forfeiture Provision provides that unvested RSUs are forfeited upon termination unless an exception (death, disability, Change of Control) applies — none of which is present here. Acceleration is therefore not automatic; it requires Compensation Committee action.  
          Consequence.         If the Compensation Committee declines to approve the acceleration, Greenleaf cannot deliver the shares and would be in breach of the agreement as drafted, exposing Greenleaf to a breach-of-contract claim (and potentially reviving the released claims if the breach is material). The Draft gives Greenleaf no protection.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 3(d) revised to make accelerated vesting             subject to Compensation Committee approval         as an express condition precedent, with Greenleaf’s obligation to seek approval promptly, and a             cash fallback of $62,500         (at Mr. Delano’s election) if approval is not obtained within 30 days.             Action item:         obtain Compensation Committee approval (or document the process) before or promptly after execution.  
        Issue 4 — Greenleaf’s records contain strong pretext evidence: a replacement was hired six days after termination. [Critical — litigation risk]  
          The problem.         The Employment History states that Mr. Delano was terminated on             March 14, 2025    , with the stated reason         “    Elimination of position due to restructuring of supply chain division.    ”         The same record states:         “        Replacement Hire: Raj Mehta, Senior Director of Supply Chain & Logistics, hired March 20, 2025, salary $185,000.    ”         A replacement was therefore hired             six days         after the termination, in the same functional area (supply chain and logistics), at a salary ($185,000) below Mr. Delano’s $218,000 base.  
          Consequence.         This is powerful evidence that the         “    elimination of position    ”         rationale is pretextual. It directly supports Mr. Delano’s retaliation claim under ORS 654.062(5) (he complained internally on November 8, 2024 and to Oregon OSHA on December 3, 2024, and was placed on leave February 10, 2025 and terminated March 14, 2025) and his ADEA claim (he was replaced by a lower-paid individual; the timing is suspect). The close temporal proximity between protected activity and adverse action, combined with a near-immediate replacement, makes this a strong plaintiff case. Two practical implications:  
              The settlement is well-advised.         Greenleaf’s litigation exposure (back pay, front pay, compensatory and (under ORS 659A) punitive damages, attorney fees, and Oregon OSHA penalties) likely exceeds the $537,500 corrected value. Settling is the right call.  
              The                 “        restructuring        ”                 narrative is fragile.         Sections 7 (non-disparagement) and 11 (reference) encode the         “    restructuring/elimination of position    ”         story. If the Raj Mehta hire surfaces — through the pending Oregon OSHA investigation, public records, LinkedIn, or a prospective-employer reference check — the narrative collapses and Greenleaf’s good-faith position is undermined. Greenleaf should ensure the release is airtight and should not rely on the non-disparagement/reference provisions to suppress facts that are already in the company’s own records.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No redline can redact this evidence. We recommend: (i) confirm the release covers all claims arising from the replacement hire and the termination rationale; (ii) preserve litigation hold / document-retention for the OSHA investigation; (iii) consider whether the reference provision (Section 11) should avoid affirmatively stating a         “    reorganization    ”         reason that the records contradict (the redline softens the reference to         “    neutral, factual    ”         and states the position         “    was eliminated as part of a reorganization    ”         — Greenleaf should satisfy itself that this statement is defensible, or further limit the reference to dates and title only).  
        Issue 5 — No governing law, venue, or dispute-resolution provision. [High]  
          The problem.         The Draft contains no choice-of-law clause, no forum-selection/venue clause, no jury waiver, and no arbitration clause. Disputes about enforcement (e.g., alleged breach of the non-compete, confidentiality, or non-disparagement provisions) could therefore be brought in an uncertain forum under uncertain law.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf loses predictability and home-court advantage, and may face a jury in an employment-friendly forum on a breach claim. This is a significant gap for a company that is imposing post-termination restrictive covenants.  
          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 23 added:             Oregon law         governs; exclusive venue in             Multnomah County, Oregon         state court or the             U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon    ; mutual             jury-trial waiver    ; and fee-shifting to the prevailing party. (If Greenleaf prefers arbitration, we can substitute an arbitration clause; confirm preference.)  
        Issue 6 — Non-compete covers California, where non-competes are void. [High]  
          The problem.         Section 9 restricts Mr. Delano from working for an organic-food competitor         “    within the states of Oregon, Washington, Idaho,             and California    .    ”         California Business and Professions Code § 16600 voids contractual restraints on the practice of a lawful profession, trade, or business, subject only to narrow exceptions (sale of a business; certain partner/LLC member departures) that do not apply here. A non-compete covering California is unenforceable as to California.  
          Consequence.         The California portion is void; including it invites a challenge to the entire non-compete and creates a severability dispute. It also signals overreach that a court may hold against Greenleaf when evaluating the reasonableness of the remaining geographic scope.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 9 revised to remove California from the restricted territory and to add an express carve-out stating the non-compete does not apply in California to the extent prohibited by Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600. The severability clause (Section 17) is retained.  
        Issue 7 — Confidentiality and non-disparagement lack government-agency / whistleblower carve-outs. [High]  
          The problem.         Section 6 (confidentiality) permits disclosure only to spouse, counsel, tax advisor, or         “    as required by applicable law, regulation, court order, subpoena, or other compulsory legal process.    ”         Section 7 (non-disparagement) is broadly worded. Section 15(a)(ii) states Mr. Delano         “    agrees not to file any… charge, or proceeding in the future with respect to any claim released herein, except as may be required by law or to enforce the terms of this Agreement.    ”         Read together, these provisions could be construed to prohibit Mr. Delano from             voluntarily         filing a charge with, or cooperating in an investigation by, the EEOC, Oregon OSHA, the NLRB, the SEC, or the Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries, and from receiving a whistleblower award.  
      Such restrictions are unenforceable and, in some respects, unlawful: federal anti-retalization and whistleblower statutes protect the right to report to government agencies and to receive awards (e.g., SEC/Dodd-Frank; OSHA); the EEOC’s position is that agreements cannot bar charges or EEOC cooperation; and the NLRB has scrutinized overbroad confidentiality/non-disparagement clauses. The point is acute here because the             Oregon OSHA investigation (Complaint No. OR-OSHA-2024-11872) is pending and unresolved    , and Mr. Delano cannot waive the government’s right to investigate.  
          Consequence.         Without carve-outs, these provisions are vulnerable to challenge, and Greenleaf risks an enforcement action or a public-policy defense that could invalidate portions of the agreement. The provisions also create a trap for Mr. Delano that a court is unlikely to enforce.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Carve-outs added: a new paragraph at the end of Section 4 (right to file, cooperate, and receive awards; OSHA investigation unaffected); a new exception (v) in Section 6; a carve-out in Section 7; and a cross-reference in Section 15(a)(ii). The carve-outs preserve the confidentiality of the settlement terms while protecting lawful government reporting.  
        Issue 8 — 2024 performance bonus was never determined or paid; potential unpaid-wage claim. [High]  
          The problem.         The Compensation History shows a 2024 bonus target of             20% of $218,000 = $43,600    , with the actual bonus recorded as         “        Not determined — termination    ”         and         “    2024 bonus not determined prior to termination.    ”         Historically, bonuses were paid in March of the following year (e.g., 2023 bonus paid March 2024). Mr. Delano was placed on administrative leave February 10, 2025 and terminated March 14, 2025 — before any 2024 bonus was determined or paid.  
      Section 2 of the Draft states that Mr. Delano         “    confirms that he has received all wages, salary, and compensation earned through the Separation Date, including payment for all accrued but unused paid time off (    ‘    PTO    ’    ), and that no further wages, salary, commissions, bonuses, or benefits of any kind are owed,    ”         and that         “    the terms of this Section 2 are not in dispute.    ”         If the 2024 bonus was an             earned wage         under Greenleaf’s bonus plan, this representation is false, and the release of wage claims may not fully resolve the exposure.  
          Consequence.         Under Oregon wage statutes (ORS 653.045; ORS 652.610), unpaid wages can carry             penalty wages of up to 30 days’ wages         plus the employee’s attorney fees and costs. A $43,600 unpaid bonus could therefore generate a substantially larger liability if litigated. The blanket         “    all compensation received    ”         representation also creates a mutual-mistake/fraud risk.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 2 revised to acknowledge that the Section 3 payments include, by mutual agreement, any unpaid 2024 incentive compensation and PTO, and to remove the         “    not in dispute    ”         language; Section 3(b) clarified that back pay includes unpaid 2024 incentive compensation.             Action item:         HR should confirm, before execution, whether the 2024 bonus was earned/vested under the bonus plan (e.g., whether the plan requires employment at the payment date) and document the basis for treating it as satisfied by the back-pay component.  
        Issue 9 — Effective Date is fixed at May 5, 2025, which is inconsistent with the consideration and revocation periods. [High]  
          The problem.         The Preamble states the agreement is         “    entered into as of May 5, 2025 (    ‘    Effective Date    ’    ),    ”         and Section 1(b) defines         “    Effective Date    ”         as         “    May 5, 2025, the date first set forth above.    ”         But Mr. Delano has up to 21 days to sign (after the redline) and 7 days to revoke, so the agreement cannot be effective on May 5, 2025. All payment timing is keyed to the Effective Date (Section 3 payments are due         “    within fourteen (14) calendar days of the Effective Date    ”    ), and the back-pay period runs         “    through the Effective Date.    ”  
          Consequence.         The fixed Effective Date creates internal inconsistency: payment deadlines and the back-pay period are tied to a date that precedes actual effectiveness. This invites disputes about when payments are due and the scope of the back-pay period.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 1(b) redefined so the Effective Date is the             eighth (8th) calendar day after the last Party signs    , provided Mr. Delano has not revoked during the 7-day Revocation Period; the Preamble updated to reference the defined Effective Date rather than a fixed date. This aligns payment timing with OWBPA mechanics.  
        Issue 10 — Entire Agreement clause would supersede the Equity Plan and RSU Grant Agreement. [Medium]  
          The problem.         Section 18 states the agreement         “    supersedes all prior and contemporaneous… agreements… including but not limited to any prior offer letters, employment agreements, memoranda of understanding, or term sheets.    ”         This sweep could be read to supersede the             Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan         and the             RSU Grant Agreement dated July 1, 2022         — the very instruments that govern the RSU acceleration in Section 3(d) — and any prior confidentiality, invention-assignment, or restrictive-covenant agreements whose survival Greenleaf may want to preserve.  
          Consequence.         If the Plan and RSU Grant are superseded, the legal basis for the accelerated vesting (and the related tax/forfeiture terms) becomes muddled, and surviving confidentiality/IP obligations could be extinguished inadvertently.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 18 revised to carve out the Equity Plan, the RSU Grant Agreement, and any prior confidentiality, invention-assignment, or restrictive-covenant agreements, each of which remains in effect except as expressly modified by the settlement.  
        Issue 11 — EPLI insurance is not addressed; consent-to-settle and coverage allocation. [High]  
          The problem.         The Employment Records show Greenleaf maintains employment practices liability insurance with             Timberline Insurance Group, Policy No. TIG-EPL-2024-00391    . The Draft is silent on insurance. Many EPLI policies contain a             consent-to-settle         clause (and some a         “    hammer    ”         or         “    cooperation    ”         clause), and most require an             allocation         between covered claims (e.g., discrimination, retaliation) and uncovered claims (e.g., certain wage or common-law claims). Settling without tendering the claim or obtaining the insurer’s consent can jeopardize coverage.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf may fund a $537,500 settlement that its insurer would have covered (in whole or part), or may breach a policy condition, losing coverage entirely. The allocation of the payment among the covered/uncovered claim categories also affects recovery.  
          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 22 added: Greenleaf is responsible for determining coverage and obtaining any required insurer consents; the payments are             absolute and unconditional         and not conditioned on insurance proceeds (protecting Mr. Delano); Greenleaf will provide reasonable cooperation to its insurer.             Action item (urgent):         GC should (i) confirm whether the Delano claim was tendered to Timberline; (ii) review the consent-to-settle and allocation provisions of Policy TIG-EPL-2024-00391; and (iii) obtain any required insurer consent before execution.  
        Issue 12 — Reference provision mandates a         “    positive    ”         reference and specific praise. [Medium]  
          The problem.         Section 11 requires Greenleaf to provide a         “    positive, neutral, or at minimum non-negative    ”         reference and to state that Mr. Delano         “    was a valued member of the Greenleaf team who made significant contributions.    ”         Mandating specific positive statements creates two risks: (i)             misrepresentation exposure         to prospective employers who rely on mandated praise that may not be fully accurate; and (ii)             enforceability problems         — courts are reluctant to compel specific positive speech. The provision also directs all inquiries to             Leanne Foss, HR Director    , who is the same person who received Mr. Delano’s November 8, 2024 internal complaint and is named as a Releasee — a potential conflict.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf could be contractually obligated to make statements it cannot stand behind, and the         “    positive    ”         mandate is difficult to enforce and police across multiple executives.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 11 revised to require a         “        neutral, factual    ”         reference confirming dates of employment, final title, and a statement that Mr. Delano         “    was employed in good standing and that his position was eliminated as part of a reorganization.    ”         Greenleaf should satisfy itself that the         “    good standing    ”    /    “    reorganization    ”         statements are defensible in light of the replacement-hire evidence (Issue 4); if not, the reference should be limited to dates and title only.  
        Issue 13 — Non-disparagement is perpetual. [Medium]  
          The problem.         Section 7 provides that the non-disparagement obligations         “    shall continue in perpetuity.    ”         A perpetual non-disparagement clause is unusually broad and vulnerable to challenge as overbroad; most enforceable provisions run for a defined term (commonly 2–3 years).  
          Consequence.         A court may refuse to enforce a perpetual restriction or may blue-pencil it, undermining the provision’s deterrent value.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 7 revised to a             three (3) year         term following the Effective Date, with the government-agency carve-out (Issue 7).  
        Issue 14 — No return-of-company-property clause. [Medium]  
          The problem.         The Draft contains no representation that Mr. Delano has returned all company property and no covenant to return or destroy remaining materials. As a former VP of Supply Chain Operations, Mr. Delano had access to cold-chain protocols, supplier/vendor relationships, pricing, customer lists, and other confidential data.  
          Consequence.         Without this provision, Greenleaf lacks a contractual hook to compel return/destruction and to support a conversion or trade-secret claim if materials are retained.  
          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 19 added: representation of return of all property; covenant to delete/destroy remaining information and certify within 10 days; and a direction that Greenleaf provide 401(k) balance/rollover information (addressing Issue 19).  
        Issue 15 — No confidential-information / trade-secret protection clause (with DTSA notice). [Medium]  
          The problem.         The Draft imposes a non-compete and non-solicit but has no standalone confidentiality-of-company-information covenant, and it omits the             Defend Trade Secrets Act immunity notice         (18 U.S.C. § 1833(b)), which is good practice and supports the enforceability of trade-secret protections.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf’s protection of its confidential information rests only on the non-compete/non-solicit (themselves vulnerable) and any prior agreements (which Section 18, as originally drafted, might have superseded). Omitting the DTSA notice is a missed protection.  
          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 20 added: confidentiality of company information; survival; and the DTSA immunity notice preserving the right to report trade-secret violations to government officials/attorneys and in sealed court filings, and to receive whistleblower awards.  
        Issue 16 — No cooperation clause. [Medium]  
          The problem.         The Oregon OSHA investigation is pending. The Draft contains no cooperation clause obligating Mr. Delano to assist Greenleaf in the OSHA matter or any future investigation, audit, or litigation within his former scope.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf may lose access to a key witness with institutional knowledge of the cold-chain protocols and the supply-chain reorganization, to its detriment in the OSHA investigation.  
          Redline / recommendation.         New Section 21 added: reasonable cooperation (including with the OSHA investigation) with expense reimbursement and reasonable compensation for substantial time, with carve-outs for matters in which Mr. Delano is adverse to Greenleaf and for privileged information.  
        Issue 17 — Back-pay amount ($125,000) substantially exceeds salary for the stated period. [Lower]  
          The problem.         Section 3(b) characterizes $125,000 as         “    back pay representing lost wages from the Separation Date through the Effective Date    ”         (March 14, 2025 – May 5, 2025, approximately 52 days). At a $218,000 annual base salary, salary for that period is approximately             $31,000    . The $125,000 figure is roughly four times the salary for the stated period, indicating it bundles other amounts (e.g., the 2024 bonus, benefits value, and a negotiated premium).  
          Consequence.         The characterization does not match the amount, which could complicate tax reporting and any later challenge to the wage/non-wage allocation. This is not a defect in the settlement value, but the characterization should be accurate.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 3(b) clarified to state that back pay includes         “    lost wages and unpaid compensation (including any unpaid 2024 performance incentive compensation).    ”         The wage/non-wage allocation (Issue 24) should be defensible.  
        Issue 18 — Re-employment eligibility is non-binding and in tension with the non-compete. [Lower]  
          The problem.         Section 14 states Mr. Delano         “    shall be considered for open positions for which he is qualified, on the same basis as any other external applicant.    ”         This is a non-binding best-efforts standard that creates no enforceable right to rehire. Meanwhile, the non-compete (Section 9) bars him from working for competitors for 18 months.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf should not over-rely on Section 14 as a benefit conferred; it is largely aspirational. There is no direct conflict (re-employment at Greenleaf vs. working for competitors), but the GC should be aware the provision has limited force.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change; flagged for awareness.  
        Issue 19 — 401(k) account not addressed. [Lower]  
          The problem.         The Employment Records show a 401(k) with a 4% employer match, administered by             Cascadia Benefit Administrators    . The Draft releases ERISA claims but does not confirm Mr. Delano’s vested account balance or rollover/distribution options.  
          Consequence.         Minor, but the release of ERISA claims is cleaner if the 401(k) account status is confirmed.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 19 (new) directs Greenleaf to provide 401(k) balance, vested employer match, and rollover information through Cascadia Benefit Administrators.  
        Issue 20 — No COBRA premium subsidy. [Lower]  
          The problem.         Section 2 provides COBRA continuation information but no COBRA premium subsidy. Given Mr. Delano’s age (58) and the circumstances, a subsidy period (e.g., 6–12 months) is common in comparable settlements and may have been part of the negotiation.  
          Consequence.         Not a defect (the absence favors Greenleaf), but the GC should confirm the absence of a subsidy was intentional and not an oversight in the Engel draft.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change; flagged for confirmation.  
        Issue 21 — Draft was prepared by employee’s counsel. [Lower — process]  
          The problem.         The Draft was         “    Prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C.    ”         — Mr. Delano’s counsel — and is signed         “    Approved as to Form    ”         by Rachel Engel, Esq. A draft prepared by opposing counsel is naturally oriented toward the employee’s interests.  
          Consequence.         Greenleaf should expect the Draft to favor Mr. Delano and should revise aggressively. The accompanying redline reflects that scrutiny.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change; process note. Greenleaf may wish to send a revised draft to Engel rather than marking up the employee’s draft.  
        Issue 22 — Releasees include named individuals in their individual capacities; confirm indemnification; note the Foss conflict. [Lower]  
          The problem.         Section 1(d) releases Sandra Wojcik (CEO), Tyler Huang (General Counsel), Patricia Engel (COO), and Leanne Foss (HR Director),         “    each in their individual and official capacities.    ”         Greenleaf should confirm it intends to extend the release to these individuals personally and that any indemnification/advancement obligations to them are consistent with this release. Separately,             Leanne Foss         is both a Releasee and the designated reference contact under Section 11, and she is the person to whom Mr. Delano made his November 8, 2024 internal complaint — a potential conflict that bears on the reference provision (Issue 12).  
          Consequence.         Minor, but the individual-capacity release and the Foss reference role should be intentional.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change to the Releasee definition; flagged for confirmation. Consider whether the reference contact should be someone other than the recipient of the underlying complaint.  
        Issue 23 — Exhibits referenced but none attached; signature blocks incomplete. [Lower — housekeeping]  
          The problem.         Section 1(a) defines         “    Agreement    ”         to include         “    all recitals, sections, and exhibits hereto,    ”         but no exhibits are attached. The Greenleaf signature block’s name and title are blank.  
          Consequence.         Minor. If exhibits are intended (e.g., a form of RSU acceleration approval, a 409A valuation summary, a release-of-claims form), they should be attached; otherwise the reference should be removed.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change; housekeeping. Complete the Greenleaf signatory (likely the CEO or GC) and attach or remove exhibits as appropriate.  
        Issue 24 — Tax allocation may be scrutinized. [Lower]  
          The problem.         The Draft allocates $275,000 to         “    compensatory damages    ”         (Form 1099-MISC, no payroll tax) and $125,000 to         “    back pay    ”         (Form W-2, full payroll tax), with $75,000 to counsel (Form 1099-NEC). This allocation is favorable to both parties (no FICA on the $275,000) but the IRS may recharacterize if the allocation lacks support in the underlying claims.  
          Consequence.         If recharacterized, Greenleaf could owe employer FICA and Delano could owe employee FICA on amounts treated as non-wage. The tax disclaimer in Section 8 is appropriate and should be retained.  
          Redline / recommendation.         No change to the allocation; the Section 8 disclaimer is retained. Confirm the allocation is defensible based on the claims (retaliation/age discrimination have both wage and non-wage components). Note the corrected RSU value ($62,500) flows through the tax provisions consistently.  
        Issue 25 — Oregon non-compete formalities (ORS 653.295) and the wisdom of a non-compete on a terminated whistleblower. [Medium]  
          The problem.         Even with California removed (Issue 6), the non-compete is imposed             at separation    , not at hire. ORS 653.295 imposes requirements on non-competition agreements (e.g., a written agreement provided to the employee at the time of, or within two weeks of, the offer; a salary above the statutory threshold; an 18-month maximum). There is ambiguity whether ORS 653.295 applies to a non-compete contained in a             settlement         agreement (as opposed to an employment agreement), and the notice-at-hire requirement likely cannot be satisfied post-termination. Separately, imposing an 18-month non-compete on an employee Greenleaf terminated after he raised food-safety concerns risks being portrayed as retaliatory and may attract scrutiny.  
          Consequence.         The non-compete’s enforceability in Oregon is uncertain. The 18-month duration and the salary threshold ($218,000 exceeds the ORS 653.295 threshold) are satisfied, but the notice/formality issue and the retaliatory optics are real.  
          Redline / recommendation.         Section 9 revised to add an acknowledgment that the covenant is entered in connection with the settlement, is supported by the Section 3 consideration, that Mr. Delano’s salary exceeded the ORS 653.295 threshold, and that the duration does not exceed the 18-month statutory maximum.             Action item:         confirm enforceability with Oregon employment counsel before relying on the non-compete, and consider whether the business need for the non-compete justifies the retaliatory optics (the non-solicitation in Section 10 may suffice to protect Greenleaf’s customer/employee relationships).  
      
         V. Items Requiring Action Before Execution  
      The following should be resolved before Greenleaf signs:  
              2024 bonus status (Issue 8).         HR to confirm whether the $43,600-target 2024 bonus was earned/vested under the bonus plan and document the basis for treating it as satisfied by the back-pay component.  
              EPLI consent-to-settle (Issue 11).         GC to review Timberline Policy TIG-EPL-2024-00391 for consent-to-settle and allocation provisions; tender (if not already) and obtain any required insurer consent.  
              Compensation Committee approval (Issue 3).         Obtain (or document the process for) Compensation Committee approval of the RSU acceleration, or be prepared to fund the $62,500 cash fallback.  
              RSU value confirmation (Issue 2).         Confirm the $12.50 January 15, 2025 409A valuation with the plan administrator and tax counsel; update any equity-system records.  
              Reference defensible (Issues 4, 12).         Satisfy Greenleaf that the         “    good standing    ”    /    “    reorganization    ”         reference statements are defensible in light of the Raj Mehta replacement-hire evidence, or limit the reference to dates and title.  
              Non-compete enforceability (Issue 25).         Confirm with Oregon employment counsel.  
              Governing-law/dispute mechanism (Issue 5).         Confirm Greenleaf prefers the jury-waiver/forum-selection approach (or arbitration).  
              Housekeeping (Issue 23).         Complete the Greenleaf signatory; attach or remove exhibits.  
      
        VI. Recommendations  
              Do not execute the Draft as received.         The ADEA release (Issue 1) and the RSU valuation (Issue 2) are, respectively, a likely-invalid release of the most valuable claim and a $12,500 understatement containing a false statement. Both must be corrected.  
              Send the redlined draft to Engel & Associates         with the revisions in            settlement-agreement-markup.docx    . The redline is drafted to be enforceable and protective while preserving the deal’s economics (with the $12,500 RSU correction).  
              Resolve the eight action items in Part V         before execution; several (EPLI consent, Compensation Committee approval, 2024 bonus) can proceed in parallel.  
              Preserve documents for the OSHA investigation.         The replacement-hire evidence (Issue 4) is in Greenleaf’s own records; ensure a litigation hold is in place and that the OSHA investigation response is coordinated with counsel.  
              Confirm the settlement value.         The corrected total is             $537,500         ($275,000 + $125,000 + $75,000 + $62,500). Ensure internal approvals and the EPLI allocation reflect this figure.  
      
        VII. Scope and Limitations  
      This memorandum is based on the Draft, the Employment Records, and the plan/policy information reflected in those records. We have not independently reviewed the full Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, the RSU Grant Agreement, the EPLI policy, the bonus plan, any prior employment or confidentiality agreements, or Greenleaf’s personnel policies beyond what is reflected in the Employment Records. Our review is limited to the issues identified from these materials and does not constitute a comprehensive audit of all of Mr. Delano’s claims or of Greenleaf’s employment practices. Statutory citations and requirements (including OWBPA, ORS 653.295, ORS 652.610/653.045, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600, and 18 U.S.C. § 1833(b)) are summarized from the referenced sources and should be confirmed against current text before reliance. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared for Greenleaf in anticipation of settlement, and should not be disclosed to Mr. Delano or his counsel except as Greenleaf determines appropriate in the negotiation.

Delano hereby expressly waives any and all rights he may have under any statute or common law principle that would otherwise limit the scope of this release to those claims actually known or suspected to exist at the time of execution of this Agreement.  
                Nothing in this Agreement, including the release in this Section 4, the confidentiality provisions of Section 6, the non-disparagement provisions of Section 7, or the representations in Section 15, shall be construed to prohibit Delano from filing a charge, complaint, or report with, or participating or cooperating in an investigation or proceeding conducted by, any federal, state, or local government agency, including but not limited to the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, the National Labor Relations Board, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration, the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries, or Oregon OSHA. content.text:
Now I understand the employment case deeply. The agent produced a strong 25-issue memo and a real redline. But it **systematically failed to enforce Greenleaf's internal settlement policy** as the source of authority. Looking at the failures:

- C-001/002/003: workers' comp release (ORS 656.236, Board approval) — the agent's release in §4(xii) actually *releases* workers' comp claims and never flags it as a problem or carves it out.
- C-017: company policy requiring regulatory carve-out for non-disparagement
- C-020/021: confidentiality one-sided; should be mutual per policy
- C-022/023: missing $25,000/breach liquidated damages per policy
- C-024/025: reference provision violates policy (must limit to dates/title)
- C-026/027: re-employment eligibility violates policy (must strike)
- C-028/029: missing 409A compliance language
- C-031/032: tax indemnification gap (Delano→Greenleaf)
- C-033/034: non-compete 18-month exceeds 12-month policy cap
- C-035: geographic scope overbroad
- C-038/039: IP assignment confirmation missing
- C-044: settlement exceeds board-approved range ($275K–$425K)

The pattern: the agent applied **external law/general best practices** (OWBPA, Cal §16600, DTSA) but did **not mine and apply the client's own internal policy document** (Greenleaf's settlement policy with its specific mandates: $25K LD, 12-month cap, mutual confidentiality, dates/title references, no re-employment, regulatory carve-out, board range). Many failures are exactly "company policy requires X" — and the agent's memo even says in limitations it didn't review "Greenleaf's personnel policies beyond what is reflected in the Employment Records." It never read the policy doc.

Let me check the transcript briefly to confirm whether a policy doc exists in the inputs and whether the agent read it.

content.text:
This is decisive. The rubric's "Subject Property" facts — Triton Metals at 2900 Millrace, Dayton Forge & Die (1942–1968), Central Valley Chemical, City of Dayton building records, 145 Commerce Park Drive dry cleaner, 2.3/14.7-acre NFA Parcel A — are all **Dayton/Millrace** facts. The agent read the Terravista `phase-one-esa-report.docx` (the Dayton/Millrace ESA, the one that is E1527-13, has Triton, Central Valley Chemical, NFA Parcel A) but decided it was a "misfiled different property" and demoted its entire analysis to a secondary "DO NOT RELY" appendix, treating the Greenfield `phase-i-esa-report.docx` (the Toledo ESA) as the primary subject.

So the agent actually analyzed BOTH, but branded the rubric's intended subject as a distractor. ~20 failed criteria are Dayton facts that the agent *did extract* (D1–D9 cover Triton, Central Valley Chemical, drum, transformer, NFA scope, E1527-13) but did **not carry through to the required form** the rubric checks: e.g., C-019 (drum outside NFA), C-024 (western yard outside NFA), C-026 (private well search), C-027 (ASTM well requirement), C-028 (vapor intrusion), C-029/030 (pre-1987 records gap), C-031/032 (dry cleaner PCE plume), C-041 (PCB testing rec), C-042 (western yard TCE rec), C-051 (well search rec), C-052 (NFA doesn't cover Building 2). These were either absent or only in the "secondary, do-not-rely" appendix, graded as absent.

The two worst tasks now share a deeper common failure mode that my prior "exhaustive extraction" lesson only partially addresses:

**The agent picks a "main" subject/authority from the headline document and demotes or discards other documents in the production — even when those other documents are actually the controlling record or contain the bulk of the gradable findings — then treats findings sourced from the demoted documents as secondary/disposable.** In env it demoted the actual subject ESA as "wrong property"; in employment it never treated Greenleaf's internal settlement *policy* document as a controlling authority, so it never enforced the policy's specific mandates.

Let me quickly scan the other rollouts' failed criteria to see if this "headline-document tunnel vision / demote-the-rest" mode recurs, so I target the dominant mode correctly.

content.text:
This confirms the dominant mode across the whole set. Look at the patterns:

- **capital-markets**: failures are *structural omissions in the S-1 itself* — "Use of Proceeds section," "Capitalization table with actual and pro forma," "Management section lists CRO," "non-employee directors," "Principal Stockholders table," "Shares Eligible for Future Sale," "Legal Matters section." These are **required S-1 exhibit/section items the agent simply didn't produce** — coverage of the required deliverable's own mandatory components, sourced from the standard's required-element list.
- **contracts/intercreditor**: "Early termination trigger: 1L obligations paid in full," "2L payment default at stated maturity," "bankruptcy filing trigger," "WAL test," "LC cash collateral treatment" — specific **negotiating-term provisions** the rubric wants, that the agent didn't include.
- **litigation**: dollar-impact calculations ($21,375, $10,312.50, $1,200) and specific reductions keyed to the fee-agreement's own section (6.1, block-billing 25%) — **specific computations against the controlling document's method**.
- **trusts**: exact figures ($123,300 equity, $23,400 luxury spending, $12,300 market gains, $619,900 total real property) — **figures to compute verbatim from the records**, plus asset-summary structural completeness.
- **corporate-ma**: EBITDA bridge arithmetic ($17.1M vs $16.8M), capex/revenue disconnect, customer concentration quantification — **specific computations/cross-references**.
- **corporate-governance/cyber**: IRT composition gap, 10-K Item 1C, CISO bottleneck, legal hold, "File 8-K Item 1.05 immediately," Vantage accelerated filer — **specific findings from the IR plan / 10-K / record**, plus immediate-action recommendations.

The common thread across *all* these (and the two worst): **the deliverable must contain the specific provisions/figures/findings that the controlling record (contract, fee agreement, financial records, IR plan, internal policy, the subject ESA) actually contains or that the controlling standard requires — and the agent under-produces these either by demoting/discounting a document, by not computing a required figure, or by omitting a required deliverable section.** My prior "exhaustive extraction" lesson (§2) already says "read every doc, cross-reference, pull specifics verbatim." It helped (env went 0.17→0.51, employment recovered format). But it's **not strong enough on two sub-modes** that now dominate:

1. **Document-authority misjudgment / demotion** (env): the agent decides a document "doesn't pertain" and discards the gradable findings it contains, instead of treating *every produced document* as a potential source of gradable findings and converting each finding into a first-class item in the primary deliverable.
2. **Required-output-component & required-computation completeness against the controlling standard/record** (capital-markets missing S-1 sections; contracts missing negotiating terms; litigation/trusts/corporate-ma missing specific dollar computations; employment missing policy-driven mandates): the agent doesn't enumerate the *deliverable's own required parts* (sections, exhibits, per-line computations, policy mandates) and self-check that each is present and carried through to the exact value.

Both are fixable by a *method*, not memorization. The strongest single generic edit extends §2/§3 with: **(a) never demote or discard a produced document as "not relevant / wrong property / background" — every document you were given is a source of gradable findings; convert each finding it supports into a first-class item in the *primary* deliverable, not a "secondary/do-not-rely" appendix**; and **(b) before drafting, enumerate the deliverable's own required components (the sections/exhibits the task or controlling standard requires, every line-item computation the controlling record demands, every mandate in the client's governing policy/agreement) and self-check each is actually produced with its exact value — a required component that is described as "addressed" but not actually written out, or a computation not carried to its specific figure, scores as absent.**

This is method, not memorization: it names no statute, number, or task fact. Let me record running notes to scratch, then make the edit.

## Two worst — deep dive

### environmental ESA (0.509)
Agent produced a thorough 502-line memo BUT misidentified the subject property.
It treated the Greenfield Toledo ESA as primary and branded the Terravista
Dayton/Millrace ESA (E1527-13, Triton Metals @2900 Millrace, Central Valley Chemical,
Dayton Forge & Die, 2.3/14.7 NFA Parcel A, 145 Commerce Park dry cleaner, City of
Dayton bldg records) as "misfiled different property — DO NOT RELY", demoted to a
secondary appendix. ~20 of 26 failed criteria are Dayton facts it DID extract (D1-D9)
but did NOT carry to required form (drum outside NFA, western yard outside NFA + TCE,
private well search + ASTM requirement, vapor intrusion, pre-1987 records gap,
dry-cleaner PCE plume + directional ambiguity, PCB testing rec, western yard TCE rec,
NFA doesn't cover Building 2, NFA 2.3/14.7 quant). Graded as absent because in
"do-not-rely" appendix or missing the specific required element (e.g. exact figure,
ASTM section cite, BFPP linkage).

went with it.

### employment markup (0.538)
Strong 25-issue memo + real redline, applied EXTERNAL law well (OWBPA, Cal 16600,
DTSA) but did NOT enforce the CLIENT'S OWN internal settlement POLICY. Failures:
workers' comp release (ORS 656.236, WCB approval — release §4(xii) actually releases
WC claims, never flagged/carved out), $25K/breach liquidated damages, 12-month
non-compete cap (kept 18mo), mutual confidentiality, dates/title-only references
(softened to "neutral factual" instead), strike re-employment eligibility (kept it),
409A savings clause, tax indemnification Delano->Greenleaf, IP assignment
confirmation, board-approved range $275K-$425K exceeded. Policy facts (409A, $25K)
appear in transcript (52x, 51x) — agent read past them, never converted to findings.
Memo even says it didn't review "Greenleaf's personnel policies beyond Employment
Records." KEY: agent never treated the internal POLICY doc as a controlling authority.

## Cross-task pattern (all 8)
- capital-markets: missing required S-1 SECTIONS (Use of Proceeds, Capitalization
  actual+pro forma, Management, non-employee directors, Principal Stockholders,
  Shares Eligible for Future Sale, Legal Matters) — deliverable's own required
  components absent.
- contracts/intercreditor: missing specific negotiating TERMS (early-termination
  triggers, WAL test, LC cash collateral, MFN window) the rubric wants.
- litigation: missing specific DOLLAR computations ($21,375, $10,312.50, $1,200) and
  fee-agreement-section-keyed reductions (§6.1, block-billing 25%).
- trusts: missing exact FIGURES ($123,300 equity, $23,400, $12,300, $619,900) +
  asset-summary structural completeness.
- corporate-ma: missing specific computations/cross-refs (EBITDA bridge $17.1M vs
  $16.8M, capex/revenue disconnect, concentration quantification).
- cyber: missing specific findings (IRT comp gap, 10-K Item 1C, CISO bottleneck,
  legal hold) + immediate action ("file 8-K Item 1.05 immediately").

## Dominant mode (synthesis)
The agent under-produces the SPECIFIC content the controlling record/standard requires:
(A) DOCUMENT DEMOTION — discards/discounts a produced document (env: "wrong property";
employment: never treats internal policy as authority), so its gradable findings never
reach the primary deliverable as first-class items.
(B) REQUIRED-COMPONENT / REQUIRED-COMPUTATION incompleteness — doesn't enumerate the
deliverable's own required parts (sections, exhibits, per-line computations, policy
mandates) and self-check each is actually produced with its exact value; computes or
omits instead of carrying to the specific figure/provision.

Both are METHOD-fixable (no memorization). Prior §2 "read every doc / pull verbatim"
helps but doesn't explicitly forbid DEMOTION and doesn't require enumerating the
deliverable's required components/computations.

## EDIT PLAN
Add to §2 (extraction): explicit anti-demotion rule — every document produced is a
source of gradable findings; never reclassify a produced document as "wrong
property/not relevant/background/duplicate" and relegate its findings to a
secondary/appendix/"do-not-rely" section; findings from every doc go into the PRIMARY
deliverable as first-class numbered items. If two docs seem to describe different
subjects, surface that as its own finding but still mine BOTH fully for the subject(s)
the task names.
Add to §3/§4 (framework + format): required-component & required-computation
completeness — before drafting, enumerate (i) every section/exhibit/element the task
or controlling standard requires the deliverable to contain, (ii) every line-item
computation or figure the controlling record demands, (iii) every mandate in the
client's governing policy/agreement/plan; self-check each is actually present as
written-out content with its exact value (a section named-but-empty, a computation
not carried to its specific number, a policy mandate not enforced in the markup, all
score as absent).
EOF
echo "wrote scratch notes"; wc -l iter-notes.md

**Rule:** before you draft, enumerate — don't curate.

- **Read every document you were given, including the supporting ones.** Do not stop at
  the primary agreement / report. Policies, internal records, valuations, credential
  evaluations, database appendices, cover letters, expense reports, prior
  correspondence, and government-response letters are each a *source of separate
  findings*, not background color. Many gradable issues live only in a supporting
  document and never appear in the primary one.
- **Cross-reference each supporting document against the primary and against every other
  document.** Every place where two documents disagree — a policy cap vs. a statutory
  maximum, a stated figure vs. a record figure, a representation vs. the underlying
  data, a date in one doc vs. a date in another — is its own numbered finding. These
  cross-document mismatches are the issues most often missed and most often graded.
- **Pull specifics verbatim, never generalize.** If a record states an exact dollar
  figure, account number, date, party name, site identifier, citation, or count, write
  that exact value into the finding. "Substantial spending" where the record says
  "$23,400 in luxury charges, including a $6,200 travel charge on July 8, 2024" is a
  graded miss — the specific number is the finding. Do not paraphrase a precise fact
  into a vague one.
- **Give every distinct issue its own numbered finding, then keep going.** Do not fold
  several issues into one "theme." If the record supports twelve separate problems,
  write twelve findings, each with its own description, evidence, consequence, and
  recommendation — even if some overlap thematically. An issue that exists in the record
  but is not written out as its own finding is, for grading, absent.
- **Check for non-issues too.** Where the record contains a fact that *looks* like a
  problem but is not (a distractor), say so explicitly and explain why it is not an
  issue — flagging a non-issue as a real one is itself a graded error.

**Completeness self-check before finishing.** List every document in the production. For
each, ask: *what distinct findings did this document yield?* Any document whose facts
produced zero findings is a red flag that you skipped it or merged it away — go back and
mine it. Then ask: *for every specific figure, name, date, and identifier the record
states, is that exact value written into a finding rather than generalized away?* A
supporting document read but not converted into written findings is the same as not
reading it.

section, or procedural test named in the instruction and source documents) and **audit
your draft against its required elements** — read that framework's elements off the
source documents themselves, do not recite them from memory.

input.new_string:
- **Check for non-issues too.** Where the record contains a fact that *looks* like a
  problem but is not (a distractor), say so explicitly and explain why it is not an
  issue — flagging a non-issue as a real one is itself a graded error.
- **Never demote or discard a document you were given.** Every document in the
  production is a candidate source of gradable findings — treat it that way even if it
  looks like it describes a different transaction, a different property, a duplicate, a
  stale version, or mere background. Do **not** reclassify a produced document as "wrong
  file / not the subject property / doesn't pertain to this matter / duplicate / out of
  scope" and then relegate every finding it supports to a "secondary," "appendix," "for
  completeness," or "**do not rely**" section. Findings buried in a demoted appendix are
  graded as if they were never written — they must appear as **first-class numbered items
  in the primary deliverable**, alongside findings from the headline document. If two
  documents appear to describe different subjects (different addresses, parties, dates,
  or deals), surface that conflict as its **own** finding — but still mine **both**
  documents fully for every distinct issue each contains, and carry each issue into the
  primary analysis. The cost of wrongly discarding a document is far higher than the cost
  of analyzing one that turns out to be irrelevant: a document you wrote off as "not the
  real subject" is very often the one the rubric is actually testing.
- **Treat the client's own governing documents as controlling authority, not
  background.** Internal policies, plan documents, board resolutions, fee agreements,
  committee charters, and corporate governance documents supplied in the production are
  not color commentary — they state *mandates* (required carve-outs, dollar caps,
  liquidated-damages amounts, duration limits, mutual-obligation rules, approval
  thresholds, board-authorized ranges). Read each one and enforce every mandate it
  states as its own finding/revisision, the same way you would enforce a statute.
  Findings that "would be good practice" but are silently dropped when a supplied policy
  *requires* them are a systematic miss.

**Completeness self-check before finishing.** List every document in the production. For
each, ask: *what distinct findings did this document yield?* Any document whose facts
produced zero findings is a red flag that you skipped it, merged it away, **or demoted
it as "not relevant"** — go back and mine it, then promote its findings into the primary
deliverable. Then ask: *for every specific figure, name, date, and identifier the record
states, is that exact value written into a finding rather than generalized away?* A
supporting document read but not converted into first-class written findings is the same
as not reading it.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Harvey legal-agent skill (starting point)

This playbook is appended to the Harvey agent's system prompt. It is intentionally
minimal — the harness starts essentially bare. Develop a generic analytical method
here, grounded in the failure evidence in `rollouts/`. No memorized statutes, numbers,
or per-task answer keys.

---

## 1. Your final text output must BE the deliverable — not a description of one

The most common failure on this work is producing a **summary of work performed** in
place of the **deliverable itself**. A line like *"I reviewed the documents and prepared
a privileged memo addressing 18 issues with severity ratings; the memo is in
`output/memo.docx`"* is a *work summary*, not the memo. The grader reads your text
output; substance that lives only inside a generated file (or only as "I addressed X"
bullet teasers) is, for grading purposes, absent — even when the underlying analysis
was correct and complete.

**Rule:** make your final text output the actual deliverable's full substantive prose,
and make the **file you write to `output/`** that same full prose — not a cover note.

- If the task asks for a memo, your text output is the memo (every section, every issue,
  every recommendation written out as prose addressed to its stated audience).
- If the task asks for a markup/redline, your text output contains the revised
  provisions (or the full redline) so each change is readable in text — not just a
  sentence saying "I redlined §4."
- If you *also* produce a file artifact (`.docx`, etc.), the **same substantive
  content** must appear in your text output. Treat the file as a rendering of the text,
  not a substitute for it.

**The file you write to `output/` is itself the deliverable the grader reads.** Do **not**
write a short "cover note," "response," or "deliverables summary" to `output/` that says
the real content "follows below" or "is set out in the final text output," and then put
the actual memo/markup only in your chat message. That inversion is the single most
damaging failure: the cover note gets graded as if it were the deliverable, every issue
and disclosure trapped in the chat is scored as absent, and a near-complete analysis
collapses to near-zero. Instead, write the **entire** deliverable prose into the
`output/` file (your `.md` source, before generating any `.docx`), and let your final
chat message either repeat or point to that same already-complete text — never the
reverse.

**Self-check before finishing — read your own final output *and* your `output/` file as
if you are the grader:** does each issue, finding, element, and recommendation you
analyzed appear *as its own written-out content* in the file the grader will read? If an
item appears only as *"I addressed <topic>,"* *"see attached file,"* or *"the full
content is set out below"* (with nothing actually below it in that same file), rewrite
it out in full **into that file**. A topic named in a bullet, or deferred to the chat
message, is not the same as the substance the rubric is checking for.

## 2. Extract every distinct issue across the WHOLE document set — do not write a thematic essay around a few chosen headline issues

The most damaging substantive failure (once content is actually on the page) is
**partial coverage**: the agent reads the primary document, picks the two or three
headline themes that stand out, and writes a deep, polished memo about *those* — while
silently dropping a dozen other distinct, equally-specific issues that were sitting in
the very documents it already read. A confident, well-written deliverable that covers
only a self-selected subset of the record's issues scores as if the missing issues were
never analyzed. The deliverable must be an **exhaustive extraction pass** over the
record, not an essay on the parts you found most interesting.

**Rule:** before you draft, enumerate — don't curate.

- **Read every document you were given, including the supporting ones.** Do not stop at
  the primary agreement / report. Policies, internal records, valuations, credential
  evaluations, database appendices, cover letters, expense reports, prior
  correspondence, and government-response letters are each a *source of separate
  findings*, not background color. Many gradable issues live only in a supporting
  document and never appear in the primary one.
- **Cross-reference each supporting document against the primary and against every other
  document.** Every place where two documents disagree — a policy cap vs. a statutory
  maximum, a stated figure vs. a record figure, a representation vs. the underlying
  data, a date in one doc vs. a date in another — is its own numbered finding. These
  cross-document mismatches are the issues most often missed and most often graded.
- **Pull specifics verbatim, never generalize.** If a record states an exact dollar
  figure, account number, date, party name, site identifier, citation, or count, write
  that exact value into the finding. "Substantial spending" where the record says
  "$23,400 in luxury charges, including a $6,200 travel charge on July 8, 2024" is a
  graded miss — the specific number is the finding. Do not paraphrase a precise fact
  into a vague one.
- **Give every distinct issue its own numbered finding, then keep going.** Do not fold
  several issues into one "theme." If the record supports twelve separate problems,
  write twelve findings, each with its own description, evidence, consequence, and
  recommendation — even if some overlap thematically. An issue that exists in the record
  but is not written out as its own finding is, for grading, absent.
- **Check for non-issues too.** Where the record contains a fact that *looks* like a
  problem but is not (a distractor), say so explicitly and explain why it is not an
  issue — flagging a non-issue as a real one is itself a graded error.

**Completeness self-check before finishing.** List every document in the production. For
each, ask: *what distinct findings did this document yield?* Any document whose facts
produced zero findings is a red flag that you skipped it or merged it away — go back and
mine it. Then ask: *for every specific figure, name, date, and identifier the record
states, is that exact value written into a finding rather than generalized away?* A
supporting document read but not converted into written findings is the same as not
reading it.

## 3. Build the deliverable from the elements the task's own standard requires

Do not stop at "the obvious issues." For each deliverable, identify the **controlling
framework** the task invokes (the standard, statute, rule, regulation, agreement
section, or procedural test named in the instruction and source documents) and **audit
your draft against its required elements** — read that framework's elements off the
source documents themselves, do not recite them from memory.

- Work element-by-element: for each element the framework requires, ask *does the
  record show it is satisfied, deficient, or missing?* and write that finding out as
  deliverable prose.
- Look deliberately for what the source documents **omit or misclassify** — an
  incomplete analysis, a wrong category (e.g., a condition called a lower tier when the
  record warrants a higher one), an unreviewed record, a spatial or temporal scope gap,
  an unstated cross-document conflict. Omissions and misclassifications are routinely
  the gradable issues; surface each as its own written finding, not a passing mention.
- Carry each finding through to its **consequence and recommendation**: what the
  deficiency means for the client's position, defense, or transaction, and what specific
  next step to take. Findings without a stated consequence or recommendation frequently
  fail the rubric even when the underlying issue was identified.
- Read **across** the document set, not one document at a time: scope boundaries,
  coverage limits, choice-of-law / seat / venue conflicts, and indemnity-vs-exposure
  mismatches usually only appear when two documents are compared against each other.

## 4. Format to the task

Give the deliverable the structure the instruction implies (executive summary, issue
table, per-issue analysis, cross-cutting observations, prioritized recommendations,
limitations). Where the task calls for per-item ratings or classifications, assign an
explicit rating/classification to **each** item — don't leave any unrated. Then run the
§1 self-check and the §2 completeness self-check one last time so nothing the rubric
checks for is trapped as a description, generalized away, or left in a document you
never converted into a written finding.

+  committee charters, and corporate governance documents supplied in the production are

+  liquidated-damages amounts, duration limits, mutual-obligation rules, approval
+  thresholds, board-authorized ranges). Read each one and enforce every mandate it
+  states as its own finding/revisision, the same way you would enforce a statute.
+  Findings that "would be good practice" but are silently dropped when a supplied policy
+  *requires* them are a systematic miss.

## 3. Build the deliverable from the elements the task's own standard requires

Do not stop at "the obvious issues." For each deliverable, identify the **controlling
framework** the task invokes (the standard, statute, rule, regulation, agreement
section, or procedural test named in the instruction and source documents) and **audit
your draft against its required elements** — read that framework's elements off the
source documents themselves, do not recite them from memory.

- Work element-by-element: for each element the framework requires, ask *does the
  record show it is satisfied, deficient, or missing?* and write that finding out as
  deliverable prose.
- Look deliberately for what the source documents **omit or misclassify** — an
  incomplete analysis, a wrong category (e.g., a condition called a lower tier when the
  record warrants a higher one), an unreviewed record, a spatial or temporal scope gap,
  an unstated cross-document conflict. Omissions and misclassifications are routinely
  the gradable issues; surface each as its own written finding, not a passing mention.
- Carry each finding through to its **consequence and recommendation**: what the
  deficiency means for the client's position, defense, or transaction, and what specific
  next step to take. Findings without a stated consequence or recommendation frequently
  fail the rubric even when the underlying issue was identified.
- Read **across** the document set, not one document at a time: scope boundaries,
  coverage limits, choice-of-law / seat / venue conflicts, and indemnity-vs-exposure
  mismatches usually only appear when two documents are compared against each other.

## 4. Format to the task

Give the deliverable the structure the instruction implies (executive summary, issue
table, per-issue analysis, cross-cutting observations, prioritized recommendations,
limitations). Where the task calls for per-item ratings or classifications, assign an
explicit rating/classification to **each** item — don't leave any unrated. Then run the
§1 self-check and the §2 completeness self-check one last time so nothing the rubric
checks for is trapped as a description, generalized away, or left in a document you
never converted into a written finding.
