## 4. Format to the task — and produce every required component with its exact value

Give the deliverable the structure the instruction implies (executive summary, issue
table, per-issue analysis, cross-cutting observations, prioritized recommendations,
limitations). Where the task calls for per-item ratings or classifications, assign an
explicit rating/classification to **each** item — don't leave any unrated.

**Before you draft, enumerate the deliverable's own required components — not just the
issues.** A deliverable is not only a list of findings; it has *structural parts* the
task or the controlling standard requires it to contain, and *computations* the
controlling record requires it to perform. Missing one of these is as damaging as
missing a finding. Enumerate, and then self-check that each is actually present as
written-out content (not named in a bullet and never produced):

- **Required sections / exhibits / elements.** If the task asks for a registration
  statement, a markup, a findings memo, an invoice analysis, or a term sheet, the
  controlling form/standard/contract names the components that document must contain
  (e.g., a Use of Proceeds section, a capitalization table with actual and pro-forma
  columns, a principal-stockholders table, a management section, specific negotiating
  terms, a per-line fee adjustment table). List every required component the task or
  its governing document calls for, and confirm each is actually written out — a
  component you "intended to include," described in one line, or omitted because it
  seemed minor scores as absent.
- **Required computations and exact figures.** Where the record supplies inputs that
  the deliverable must compute (a dollar impact = hours × rate; an equity = value −
  mortgage; a bridge sum; a concentration percentage; a budget-overage percentage;
  a settlement total against a board-authorized range), carry **each** computation to
  its **specific numeric result** and write that number into the deliverable. "The
  time entries are excessive" where the rubric wants "$21,375.00 (45.0 hrs × $475)"
  is a graded miss — the calculated figure is the finding. Do not stop at the
  qualitative conclusion when a number is determinable from the record.
- **Required revisions, not just observations.** For a markup/redline task, each
  finding that calls for a contract change must produce an **actual revision** in the
  redline (struck/inserted text), not a memo paragraph saying the clause "should be
  revised." A clause flagged for a carve-out, a dollar amount, a shortened duration,
  or a deleted provision must show that exact edit applied to that exact section.

**Final self-check before finishing** — run all four: (1) the §1 check that each item
is written-out prose in the `output/` file, not a description; (2) the §2 check that
every document was mined and no document was demoted/discarded; (3) for each required
component and required computation listed above, that it is actually present with its
exact value; (4) for a markup, that every flagged issue has a corresponding actual
edit. Nothing the rubric checks for should remain trapped as a description, generalized
away, left in a demoted document, or present only as "should be revised."

---
name: no-demote-and-required-components
description: After format + extraction fixes, dominant GLM failure is (A) demoting/discarding a produced document as "not the subject / wrong property / duplicate" so its gradable findings never reach the primary deliverable, and (B) omitting the deliverable's own required components/computations the controlling record demands. Skill edit forbids demotion and requires enumerating required sections/figures/revisions.
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

**What I changed (v0, this iteration):** Extended `harness/skill/initial_skill.md` §2 with two rules and a strengthened self-check, and rewrote §4 around required-component completeness. (a) **Anti-demotion:** never reclassify a produced document as "wrong file / not the subject property / doesn't pertain / duplicate / out of scope" and relegate its findings to a "secondary / appendix / do-not-rely" section; findings from every produced doc must be first-class numbered items in the *primary* deliverable. If two docs seem to describe different subjects, surface that as its own finding but still mine BOTH fully. (b) **Governing internal docs are authority:** treat the client's own supplied policies / plan docs / board resolutions / fee agreements as controlling mandates to enforce (not background). (c) **§4 required-component & required-computation completeness:** before drafting, enumerate the deliverable's own required parts — required sections/exhibits/elements the task or governing form names, every line-item computation the record demands (carried to its specific numeric result, e.g. hours×rate, value−mortgage, % overage), and for markups every actual revision (struck/inserted text) for each flagged issue — then self-check each is present as written-out content, not "intended/described." No statutes, numbers, or answer keys; all examples generic.

**Failure mode targeted (from my analysis of `rollouts/`):** Format fixes from prior iterations are confirmed working — every current deliverable is full prose in the `output/` file. Two worst tasks both fail on a deeper coverage mode the prior "exhaustive extraction" lesson only partly hits. **environmental ESA 0.509 (26 fail):** agent wrote a thorough 502-line memo but *misidentified the subject* — treated the Greenfield Toledo ESA as primary and branded the Terravista Dayton/Millrace ESA (E1527-13; Triton Metals @2900 Millrace; Central Valley Chemical; Dayton Forge & Die; 2.3/14.7-acre NFA Parcel A; 145 Commerce Park dry cleaner; City of Dayton records) as "misfiled different property — DO NOT RELY," demoting ~20 gradable findings (drum/western-yard outside NFA, private-well search + ASTM cite, vapor intrusion, pre-1987 records gap, dry-cleaner PCE plume, PCB-testing & western-yard-TCE recs, NFA scope/Bldg-2) to a secondary appendix → graded absent. **employment markup 0.538 (24 fail):** strong memo + real redline applied external law well (OWBPA, Cal 16600, DTSA) but never enforced the client's *internal settlement policy* — missed $25K/breach liquidated damages, 12-month non-compete cap (kept 18mo), mutual confidentiality, dates/title-only references, strike re-employment eligibility, 409A clause, tax indemnification, IP-assignment confirmation, board-range exceedance; policy facts appeared in transcript (409A 52×, $25K 51×) but were never converted to findings/revisions. Cross-task: capital-markets missing required S-1 *sections*; contracts missing specific negotiating *terms*; litigation/trusts/corporate-ma missing specific *dollar computations*; cyber missing immediate-action recs. Two sub-modes: (A) document demotion, (B) required-component/computation incompleteness.

**Why it generalizes:** method, not memorization — "every produced document is a source of gradable findings; never demote one" and "enumerate the deliverable's required components/computations and self-check each is present with its exact value" apply to any matter regardless of practice area and read no task-specific facts, so they cannot force irrelevant artifacts (they say extract/enforce what the record warrants). This is the layer after [[exhaustive-extraction-not-thematic-essay]]: that fixed *reading* every doc and pulling verbatim; this fixes (i) not *discarding* a doc after reading it, and (ii) producing the deliverable's required *structure and computations*, not just its issue list. Builds on [[emit-deliverable-not-work-summary]] and [[deliverable-file-not-cover-note]] (format) which remain in force.
